title: A100 (Nvidia Ampere)
tldr: Nvidia's data-center GPU that trained GPT-3 and early Llama; now mid-tier for training, widely used for inference.
plain_english: The A100 is a GPU (graphics processor) Nvidia built specifically for AI work. Think of it as a specialized calculator that excels at the billions of mathematical operations needed to train and run large language models. It dominated from 2020–2023 because it struck a balance between raw speed and cost—and you can still buy used ones affordably.
how_it_works: The A100 has 40GB or 80GB of fast on-chip memory (HBM2e), ~2 TB/s bandwidth for moving data, and ~312 trillion floating-point operations per second (TFLOPs) in bfloat16 precision. It supports structured sparsity (skipping some math when safe), multi-instance GPU partitioning (splitting one GPU across multiple jobs), and mixed-precision training (combining fast low-precision math with slower high-precision math where it matters). You plug it into a server, load your model weights and training data, and the GPU runs the forward pass, backward pass, and weight updates faster than a CPU could.
why_it_matters: A100 remains the price-performance sweet spot for inference and fine-tuning on consumer budgets. Many smaller teams and researchers rely on secondary-market A100s because they're cheaper than H100s but still handle production workloads. Widespread deployment means strong ecosystem support (libraries, optimization tricks, hiring precedent).
example: A research team fine-tuning Llama-2 on 4× A100s can train a 7B-parameter model on a custom dataset for ~$400–600 in cloud compute or build a rack for ~$40k one-time. An inference service using A100s can batch 16+ requests per forward pass, keeping latency under 100ms while maximizing throughput—crucial for cost-conscious API providers.
pitfalls: Assuming all 40GB variants have the same memory or bandwidth—some older A100-40GB cards have lower bandwidth than 80GB versions.
Not accounting for PCIe bandwidth bottleneck when chaining multiple A100s; NVLink gives you full bandwidth, but PCIe PCIe 4.0 can starve the GPU.
Neglecting HBM2e cooling requirements in cluster design; A100s generate serious heat and need proper airflow or liquid cooling.
Treating A100 as sufficient for frontier model training today; H100s and newer chips are faster per dollar for pre-training at scale.
when_use: Use A100 for inference, fine-tuning, or academic research where cost-per-inference matters more than absolute speed; for prototyping before moving to H100s; or for workloads where used hardware is acceptable.
when_avoid: Avoid A100 for training frontier models from scratch—H100 or newer chips amortize cost better at scale. Skip it if you need sustained <50ms latency on single requests or are building a greenfield system where reliability contracts matter more than cost.

faq.q: What's the difference between A100 40GB and 80GB?
faq.a: The 80GB variant has twice the memory and higher memory bandwidth (~2.4 TB/s vs ~1.5 TB/s), making it better for huge models or longer sequences. The 40GB is cheaper and fine for most fine-tuning and inference.

faq.q: Can I use A100 for real-time inference?
faq.a: Yes, if you batch requests or accept ~50–100ms latency. A100 excels at batch inference; single-request latency is slower than newer GPUs because it's a training-focused chip.

faq.q: Is A100 still worth buying in 2024?
faq.a: For used/secondary-market units, yes—you'll pay $10k–15k for a 40GB card, making it cost-effective for inference and fine-tuning. For new purchases, H100 or L40S are better value.

faq.q: How many A100s do I need to train a 7B model?
faq.a: One A100-40GB can train a 7B model with gradient checkpointing; 2–4 A100s let you iterate faster or handle larger batches without offloading.

faq.q: What's the power consumption of an A100?
faq.a: A100 draws ~250W under full load, so a server with 8× A100s needs ~2kW peak plus infrastructure overhead—plan cooling and electrical accordingly.

title: Accelerate (Hugging Face)
tldr: Hugging Face library that runs training loops on any hardware without rewriting code for each setup.
plain_english: Accelerate is a thin wrapper around PyTorch's distributed training tools. You write your training loop once, then Accelerate handles the hardware complexity—whether you're on a single GPU, eight GPUs, or multi-node clusters—without you changing your code. It's like a universal adapter for training.
how_it_works: You decorate your model and optimizer with `accelerator.prepare()`, replace `.backward()` with `accelerator.backward()`, and launch via `accelerate launch`. Accelerate detects your hardware and automatically picks the right backend: PyTorch DDP for multi-GPU, FSDP for sharded training, DeepSpeed for advanced optimizations, or TPU drivers if available. It also handles mixed precision, gradient accumulation, and gradient clipping uniformly across backends, so your training code stays identical.
why_it_matters: Distributed training usually requires framework-specific boilerplate that breaks when you move to different hardware. Accelerate eliminates that friction, making it the default choice in Hugging Face projects and letting teams scale from laptop prototypes to multi-node runs without refactoring.
example: A researcher trains a 3B model on a single H100. When results look good, they change `accelerate launch` to run across four nodes without touching the training script. The same code handles device placement, synchronization, and gradient sharding automatically.
pitfalls: Using `accelerator.prepare()` on objects already on the correct device can cause double-wrapping; always check device placement before calling it.
Mixing manual `.to(device)` calls with Accelerate's automatic placement leads to unexpected GPU/CPU splits; let Accelerate own device movement.
Gradient accumulation steps must be coordinated with `accelerator.backward()`, or effective batch size silently diverges across runs.
DeepSpeed integration requires a config file; forgetting it falls back to DDP, losing ZeRO optimizations you expected.
when_use: Use Accelerate when you own the training loop, need to run on multiple hardware types without code changes, or want to add DeepSpeed/FSDP without restructuring.
when_avoid: Avoid if you're using a high-level trainer abstraction (e.g., HF Trainer, PyTorch Lightning) that already handles distribution, or if you need custom distributed patterns not covered by Accelerate's backends.

faq.q: Do I have to rewrite my training loop to use Accelerate?
faq.a: No. Accelerate wraps your existing PyTorch loop. You add `accelerator.prepare()` around your model/optimizer and replace `.backward()` and `.step()` calls, but the loop structure stays the same.

faq.q: Can Accelerate switch between DDP, DeepSpeed, and FSDP without code changes?
faq.a: Yes. The backend is selected at launch time via `--config_file` or environment flags. Your code doesn't know which backend is running.

faq.q: Is Accelerate the same as PyTorch Lightning or HF Trainer?
faq.a: No. Accelerate is lower-level—it handles distributed logistics but leaves training logic to you. Lightning and Trainer are full training frameworks that bundle Accelerate-like functionality plus logging, checkpointing, and validation loops.

faq.q: What happens if I forget to call accelerator.prepare()?
faq.a: Your model and optimizer won't be wrapped for distributed training, so multi-GPU launches will either fail or run only on one device, losing parallelism.

faq.q: Does Accelerate work with custom training loops that use autograd tape?
faq.a: Yes, as long as you replace `.backward()` with `accelerator.backward()`. Custom tape-based optimizers are supported.

title: ALiBi (Attention with Linear Biases)
tldr: Position encoding method that adds distance-based linear biases to attention scores instead of embedding positions.
plain_english: Instead of giving each position in a sequence its own learnable number (embedding), ALiBi just adds a penalty to attention scores based on how far apart two tokens are. The penalty grows linearly, and each attention head gets its own slope. This simple trick lets models generalize better to longer sequences than they were trained on.
how_it_works: During attention computation, after calculating query-key dot products, ALiBi subtracts a distance-dependent bias from each score. The bias is: distance × (per-head slope). The slope is a learnable parameter or fixed value specific to each head. Closer tokens get smaller penalties; distant tokens get larger ones. No position embeddings are added to the model input—the distance bias alone provides positional information. This happens at inference for any sequence length without retraining.
why_it_matters: ALiBi models extrapolate to longer sequences than training length more reliably than sinusoidal or learned position embeddings. This matters for teams building long-context systems who want predictable generalization. It's also simpler to implement and slightly faster than RoPE. Understanding ALiBi is essential when reading 2022–2023 architecture papers and evaluating trade-offs between position encoding schemes.
example: BLOOM and MPT models used ALiBi to train on 2K tokens but handle 4K or 8K inference with better perplexity than comparable sinusoidal baselines. A research team benchmarking extrapolation on a legal-document summarization task found ALiBi-based models degraded only 3–5% on 2× training length, vs. 12–15% for RoPE variants without scaling adjustments.
pitfalls: ALiBi slopes are often fixed per-head rather than trained, limiting flexibility; some implementations use learned slopes and see conflicting results.
Extrapolation works well for *moderately* longer sequences (1–2× training length) but degrades beyond that; it is not a solution for infinite context.
RoPE has become dominant since 2023 with better fine-tuning and scaling properties; ALiBi is now a niche choice unless you have strong extrapolation needs.
when_use: Use ALiBi when you need a model to generalize to longer sequences than training length, prefer simplicity over state-of-the-art performance, or are studying position encoding trade-offs in research.
when_avoid: Avoid ALiBi if you want the best benchmark performance on standard long-context tasks (RoPE is more common), need very long extrapolation (8–16× training length), or are fine-tuning existing RoPE models.

faq.q: How is ALiBi different from RoPE?
faq.a: ALiBi adds a static distance penalty after attention scores are computed; RoPE rotates query and key vectors before computing scores. RoPE scales better with fine-tuning and has become standard since 2023. ALiBi extrapolates more predictably for modest length increases but offers fewer tuning knobs.

faq.q: Can ALiBi extrapolate beyond 2x training length?
faq.a: Extrapolation works best within 1–2× training length. Beyond that, perplexity degrades significantly. ALiBi is not designed for extreme extrapolation; consider context-aware retrieval or other methods for very long sequences.

faq.q: Do I need to retrain a model to change ALiBi slopes?
faq.a: No. ALiBi slopes are applied at inference time and can be adjusted without retraining. Some implementations allow interpolation or extrapolation of slopes, though this is an open research area.

faq.q: Is ALiBi still used in modern models?
faq.a: Rarely. MPT and BLOOM used it; most new models use RoPE. ALiBi is worth studying for architecture comparison and research into positional encoding, but it is not a default choice for production models.

title: Anthropic Workbench / Console
tldr: Web IDE for iterating Claude prompts, testing variants, and managing API keys without writing code.
plain_english: Anthropic's Workbench is a browser-based interface where you write and refine prompts with Claude, see how the model responds, and tweak your wording until you get the output you want. Think of it like a notepad that talks back. Once you're happy with a prompt, you can save it and call it from your application.
how_it_works: You log into the Anthropic Console, open a new Workbench session, and type your prompt into the left panel. Claude responds in the right panel. You can test multiple prompt variants side-by-side, adjust parameters like temperature and token limits, and inspect response details including token counts and cache hits. Saved prompts are stored in your Prompt Library and can be invoked via the API using the prompt ID. The Console also displays your API key, usage analytics, and billing.
why_it_matters: Most Claude development starts in Workbench because iteration is faster and cheaper than code-based trial-and-error. You get immediate feedback without deploying, and you reduce token waste by refining offline. For teams, it's the first place you explore what Claude can do before engineering effort.
example: A customer support team at a fintech startup uses Workbench to test different system prompts for a dispute-resolution agent. They discover that adding 'think step-by-step' increases accuracy by 12%. They save this prompt to Prompt Library, reference it in their API calls, and monitor performance in the analytics dashboard—all without touching code.
pitfalls: Confusing Workbench testing with production: low-volume manual tests don't catch scaling or concurrency issues.
Not enabling prompt caching when reusing the same system prompt repeatedly; you miss 90% cost savings.
Overrelying on Workbench metrics (like perplexity) instead of measuring actual end-user outcomes.
Saving dozens of prompt variants without versioning or naming convention; later you can't tell them apart.
when_use: Use Workbench when starting a new Claude project, exploring model capabilities, or refining a prompt before deployment.
when_avoid: Avoid Workbench for production troubleshooting; use API logs and monitoring tools instead. Don't use it to simulate high-load scenarios.

faq.q: What's the difference between Anthropic Workbench and OpenAI Playground?
faq.a: Both let you test prompts interactively. Workbench is tightly integrated with Anthropic's Prompt Library and caching features; Playground is OpenAI's equivalent but works with GPT models. Feature parity varies—Workbench shows token costs per request, Playground emphasizes model selection.

faq.q: Can I save and reuse prompts from Workbench in my API code?
faq.a: Yes. Save a prompt as a Prompt Library entry in Workbench, get its ID, and reference it in your API calls. This avoids duplicating the prompt in code and makes version management easier.

faq.q: Does using Workbench count toward my API billing?
faq.a: Yes. Every prompt and response in Workbench consumes tokens just like API calls. The Console shows per-request token counts so you can track spend.

faq.q: Can I collaborate with teammates in Workbench?
faq.a: Workbench is per-user; you can't edit the same session simultaneously. Share prompts by exporting from Prompt Library or copying the prompt text, then import into a teammate's Workbench.

faq.q: How do I enable prompt caching in Workbench?
faq.a: Caching is available in Workbench if your plan supports it. Long system prompts are automatically cached; you'll see cache hit rates in the response metadata. No extra setup needed.

title: AutoGen
tldr: Microsoft framework for orchestrating multi-agent conversations where agents with distinct roles solve tasks through structured dialogue.
plain_english: AutoGen lets you build a team of AI agents that talk to each other to get work done. One agent might plan a solution, another executes it, and a third checks the results. Instead of writing a single prompt to an LLM, you define multiple agents with specific jobs and let them collaborate—the framework handles the back-and-forth conversation.
how_it_works: You define agents by attaching LLM instances, system prompts (roles), and tool access to each one. AutoGen handles message routing: Agent A sends a response, Agent B receives it, processes it, and replies. Common patterns include Assistant-UserProxy (planner + executor), group chats with a moderator agent to manage turns, and hierarchical teams. The framework tracks conversation history, manages function calls, and allows code execution with result feedback—so if one agent writes Python, another can run it and report results.
why_it_matters: Multi-agent orchestration appears in most production AI systems today. AutoGen's explicit patterns (two-agent, group chat, hierarchy) have become defaults that teams reuse or build on. It separates agent definition from orchestration logic, making it easier to test and scale agent workflows without rewriting conversation plumbing.
example: A data team uses AutoGen to debug a failing ETL pipeline. Agent A (Analyst) reviews logs and proposes a fix, Agent B (Engineer) writes the SQL, and Agent C (Validator) runs the query against a test database and confirms it works. The conversation is persistent, so the team can review the entire decision tree and rerun specific steps if needed.
pitfalls: Circular conversations: agents can get stuck debating or repeating points if termination rules are vague; define explicit exit conditions per agent.
Token cost explosion: group chats repeat full history to every agent; large teams on long tasks multiply costs fast—use summaries or sliding windows.
Tool hallucination: agents may call functions that don't exist or misunderstand parameters; validate tool schemas and error handling upfront.
Determinism: AutoGen conversations depend on model temperature and sampling; use `temperature=0` and `seed` if reproducibility matters.
when_use: Use AutoGen when you need agents with distinct roles to collaborate (planning + execution, review loops), when conversation history and decision transparency matter, or when you want to avoid monolithic prompt engineering.
when_avoid: Avoid AutoGen for simple single-agent tasks, real-time interactive systems where latency is critical, or when you need extreme cost control—group chats multiply LLM calls.

faq.q: What's the difference between AutoGen and LangChain?
faq.a: LangChain is a building-block library for chaining LLM calls and tools; AutoGen is a framework for defining agents and orchestrating multi-agent conversations. LangChain is lower-level (composable); AutoGen provides pre-built patterns (conversation, group chat, hierarchies).

faq.q: Can AutoGen run agents in parallel?
faq.a: AutoGen handles sequential turn-taking by default (Agent A → B → C → repeat). Parallel execution requires custom workflow code or integration with async task runners like Ray or Celery.

faq.q: How do I prevent AutoGen agents from running forever?
faq.a: Set `max_consecutive_auto_reply` per agent, use explicit termination phrases, implement a moderator agent to call `stop` when done, or add a hard message-count limit to the conversation.

faq.q: Does AutoGen work with non-OpenAI models?
faq.a: Yes. AutoGen accepts any LLM via `llm_config` (local models via Ollama, Hugging Face, Groq, etc.); you provide the API client.

faq.q: What's the cost impact of adding more agents to a conversation?
faq.a: Each agent receives the full conversation history; adding agents increases tokens per turn (and per model call). A 5-turn conversation with 3 agents costs roughly 3× a single-agent version.

title: Axolotl
tldr: YAML-configured LLM fine-tuning framework supporting SFT, LoRA, QLoRA, DPO, and multi-GPU training without custom code.
plain_english: Axolotl is a configuration-driven fine-tuning tool: you write a YAML file specifying your base model, dataset, training method, and hardware, then run a single command. Instead of writing PyTorch loops yourself, Axolotl handles the integration of Transformers, TRL, DeepSpeed, and distributed training under the hood. It ships with templates for common scenarios (ChatML, ShareGPT, Alpaca formats) so you iterate faster.
how_it_works: You define your training setup in YAML: base model path, dataset format (Alpaca, ChatML, or custom), training method (full fine-tune, LoRA, QLoRA, DPO, ORPO), learning rate, batch size, and hardware (GPUs, multi-node). Axolotl reads the config and orchestrates data loading, tokenization, gradient checkpointing, and distributed training via FSDP or DeepSpeed. It logs metrics and saves checkpoints, then you convert the output to GGUF or Hugging Face format for inference.
why_it_matters: Fine-tuning production LLMs normally requires weeks of boilerplate: custom data loaders, distributed training setup, PEFT integration, and hardware scaling. Axolotl collapses that into config changes, cutting setup time from days to hours and letting teams run multiple experiments without rebuilding training loops each time.
example: A team at a mid-size fintech firm needs to fine-tune Llama-2-7B on 50K in-house banking Q&A pairs using LoRA on 4×H100s. They clone Axolotl, copy the LoRA template config, update the dataset path and output directory, run `axolotl train config.yaml`, and get a production-ready adapter in 12 hours. Adding DPO training the next day is a single YAML field change.
pitfalls: Confusing Axolotl's YAML config syntax across versions can cause silent failures; always validate config before long training runs.
Multi-GPU training requires correct FSDP or DeepSpeed settings; wrong sharding strategy wastes VRAM or fails partway through.
Dataset format mismatches (e.g., wrong ChatML template) silently produce corrupted training data; validate a small sample first.
Axolotl abstracts away low-level control; if you need custom loss functions or exotic PEFT variants, you'll hit the framework's boundaries.
when_use: Use Axolotl when you're fine-tuning multiple models or methods iteratively, running on single or multi-GPU setups, and want to avoid writing training loops; it's fastest for standard SFT, LoRA, and DPO workflows.
when_avoid: Avoid Axolotl if you need custom training loops, novel loss functions, or research-grade control over gradient updates; use raw PyTorch or JAX instead.

faq.q: What is Axolotl fine-tuning?
faq.a: Axolotl is a CLI tool that wraps Hugging Face Transformers and training libraries under a single YAML config. Instead of writing Python training code, you define your model, data, and method in YAML and run it.

faq.q: Is Axolotl the same as Unsloth?
faq.a: No. Unsloth optimizes training speed through kernel-level changes; Axolotl is a configuration framework that can *use* Unsloth under the hood. Axolotl handles orchestration; Unsloth handles performance.

faq.q: Can Axolotl handle multi-GPU and multi-node training?
faq.a: Yes. Axolotl integrates FSDP and DeepSpeed for distributed training. You specify GPU count and sharding strategy in YAML.

faq.q: Does Axolotl work with open-source models?
faq.a: Yes. Axolotl works with any Hugging Face model: Llama, Mistral, Qwen, etc. It does not work with closed-source APIs like GPT-4.

faq.q: What training methods does Axolotl support?
faq.a: SFT (supervised fine-tune), LoRA, QLoRA, DPO, ORPO, and full parameter fine-tuning. It also supports continued pretraining and instruction tuning workflows.

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes. Axolotl integrates FSDP and DeepSpeed for distributed training. You specify GPU count and sharding strategy in YAML.

mainEntity.name: Does Axolotl work with open-source models?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes. Axolotl works with any Hugging Face model: Llama, Mistral, Qwen, etc. It does not work with closed-source APIs like GPT-4.

mainEntity.name: What training methods does Axolotl support?

acceptedAnswer.text: SFT (supervised fine-tune), LoRA, QLoRA, DPO, ORPO, and full parameter fine-tuning. It also supports continued pretraining and instruction tuning workflows.

title: B200 (Nvidia Blackwell)
tldr: Nvidia's Blackwell GPU (2024+) with 192GB HBM3e memory, 8 TB/s bandwidth, and FP4 support; roughly 2× H100 throughput.
plain_english: The B200 is Nvidia's newest training GPU. Think of it as an H100 with twice the speed, much larger on-chip memory, and the ability to train using ultra-low-precision numbers (FP4). It's what frontier AI labs buy when they need to train the next generation of large models.
how_it_works: B200 pairs a Blackwell-architecture processor with 192GB of HBM3e memory running at ~8 TB/s bandwidth. It delivers ~20 PFLOPs in FP4 mode and ~5 PFLOPs in FP8. NVLink 5.0 connects two B200s in a GB200 system, reaching 1.8 TB/s intra-node bandwidth. The larger memory and bandwidth reduce bottlenecks during long-sequence training; FP4 support lets teams experiment with lower precision without accuracy loss.
why_it_matters: B200 defines the current training frontier. Top labs budget for it because the performance jump justifies the cost for large-scale model work. Access to B200 availability often gates when teams can begin next-generation research.
example: Anthropic, OpenAI, and Deepseek began transitioning to Blackwell through 2024–2026. A team training a 1T-parameter model on 8× B200s completes pretraining 6–8 weeks faster than H100 equivalents, freeing GPU budget for multimodal research or longer context windows.
pitfalls: FP4 training is still new; some frameworks don't auto-cast efficiently, leaving potential on the table.
NVLink 5.0 requires specific topology setup; misconfiguration tanks all-reduce performance by 40%+.
B200 cluster cost is high enough that sub-optimal utilization (idle GPU waiting on I/O) burns budget fast.
HBM3e memory is fast but fixed; you can't add more after purchase if your model grows mid-training.
when_use: Use B200 when training frontier-scale models (100B+ parameters), running very long-context workloads (32K+ tokens), or experimenting with FP4 precision research.
when_avoid: Avoid B200 for inference-only deployments, small model fine-tuning (<50B parameters), or if your workload fits comfortably on H100/A100 and doesn't need sub-week training time.

faq.q: How much faster is B200 than H100?
faq.a: B200 delivers ~2× the FP8 throughput and up to 4× in FP4 mode. Real-world speedups vary by workload, framework, and network setup; expect 1.8–2.2× for typical pretraining.

faq.q: Can I use B200 for inference?
faq.a: Yes, but it's overkill. B200's value is in throughput and memory for training. For inference, L40S or H100 offer better cost-per-query.

faq.q: What's the difference between B200 and GB200?
faq.a: B200 is the GPU alone. GB200 is a system pairing two B200s with a Grace CPU and 1.8 TB/s NVLink 5.0 interconnect for tighter coupling in multi-GPU workloads.

faq.q: Does FP4 training work with all frameworks?
faq.a: No. PyTorch and JAX have experimental support; TensorFlow lags. Hand-coding or using custom kernels is still common for production FP4 pipelines.

faq.q: How much does a B200 cost?
faq.a: Pricing is not officially public, but industry reports indicate $30K–$40K per GPU retail. Cloud providers charge $3–$5 per GPU-hour for on-demand access.

title: BAML (Boundary AI Markup Language)
tldr: Domain-specific language for defining typed LLM functions with prompts and tests; compiles to Python or TypeScript.
plain_english: BAML lets you write LLM function definitions in a dedicated file format—similar to how you'd write a schema for a database. You specify inputs, outputs, the prompt template, and test cases. The compiler generates working code in your language of choice. Think of it as a function signature for AI: type-safe, versioned, and testable.
how_it_works: You create .baml files declaring function schemas (input fields, output structure), write the prompt template inline, and optionally add test cases with expected outputs. The BAML compiler parses these files and generates type-safe Python or TypeScript bindings. At runtime, those bindings handle LLM calls, parse responses into your defined types, and apply retry logic. Versions are tracked automatically—swap prompt versions without touching code.
why_it_matters: Scattered prompt code breeds maintenance debt: hardcoded strings, no schema validation, no test coverage. BAML centralizes prompts, enforces types across language boundaries, and makes prompt iteration explicit. For teams shipping multiple LLM functions, this reduces bugs and friction when updating prompts or retrying failed calls.
example: An e-commerce team building a product-classification LLM function writes one .baml file defining input (raw product title, description) and output (category: enum, confidence: float). They add 10 test cases. The compiler generates Python classes; developers import and call it like a normal function. When prompt tweaks improve accuracy, they bump the version in the .baml file—no code changes needed.
pitfalls: Type mismatch between .baml schema and actual LLM output—BAML enforces types but will fail if the model doesn't follow its prompt.
Prompt template logic gets complex in .baml syntax; simple conditional logic is clean, but deep branching is hard to read.
Test cases drift from real-world distribution; passing .baml tests doesn't guarantee production accuracy.
Lock-in risk: migrating away from BAML requires rewriting all prompt definitions in your target framework.
when_use: Use BAML when you have 3+ LLM functions sharing a codebase, need strong typing across Python/TypeScript, or want first-class prompt versioning and testing.
when_avoid: Avoid BAML for one-off prototypes, simple string-prompt wrappers, or if your team is unfamiliar with DSLs and syntax overhead outweighs type safety gains.

faq.q: What's the difference between BAML and Instructor?
faq.a: Instructor is a Python library that adds Pydantic models to LLM calls for type safety; BAML is a language and compiler that centralizes prompt definitions and generates bindings in multiple languages. Instructor fits into existing code; BAML replaces how you structure and version prompts.

faq.q: Does BAML lock me into a specific LLM provider?
faq.a: No. BAML compiles to code that calls standard LLM APIs (OpenAI, Anthropic, etc.). You configure which provider or model in your runtime setup; swapping providers doesn't require rewriting .baml files.

faq.q: Can I use BAML for real-time streaming responses?
faq.a: BAML's current design focuses on complete, structured responses with type parsing. Streaming is possible at the code level, but partial responses won't parse into the declared types until complete.

faq.q: Is BAML production-ready?
faq.a: BAML is mature for typed, deterministic LLM workflows. Adoption is smaller than Instructor or LangChain, so you'll have fewer StackOverflow answers and fewer integrations. Evaluate for your team's tolerance for emerging tools.

faq.q: How does BAML handle retry logic and error handling?
faq.a: BAML bakes in deterministic retries (configurable attempts and backoff) if parsing fails. You can also define fallback behaviors in the .baml file, reducing boilerplate in application code.

title: BentoML
tldr: Open-source framework that packages ML models with code and dependencies into versioned deployable artifacts.
plain_english: BentoML lets you wrap a trained model into a self-contained service definition, similar to how Docker containers bundle applications. You define inputs and outputs, bundle the model, code, and environment specs into a 'bento,' then deploy it to Kubernetes or BentoCloud with automatic scaling and batching built in.
how_it_works: You create a Python service class that loads your model and defines prediction methods with typed input/output schemas. BentoML serializes this into a bento—a versioned artifact with the model weights, service code, and dependency manifest. At runtime, BentoML spins up an HTTP/gRPC server, handles request routing, and can micro-batch requests for throughput. Deployment tools like Yatai push bentos to Kubernetes clusters or managed clouds; BentoML handles scaling and autoshutdown.
why_it_matters: Reduces friction between model development and production serving. Instead of writing Flask boilerplate or wrestling with Triton's C++ config, you stay in Python, specify schemas declaratively, and get Docker artifacts and cloud deployment out of the box. Teams ship models faster and with fewer hand-offs between ML and DevOps.
example: A credit-risk team trains a scikit-learn classifier and wraps it in a BentoML service with pydantic schemas for loan features and risk scores. They build a bento, push it to their Kubernetes cluster via Yatai, and the service auto-scales from 1 to 10 replicas based on request volume. No custom FastAPI code, no manual Docker debugging.
pitfalls: Assuming BentoML's dynamic batching is a free lunch—latency-throughput tradeoffs still apply; tune batch size and timeout explicitly.
Deploying a bento without resource limits in Kubernetes; BentoML will spawn workers up to CPU cores available, risking node thrashing.
Using Bento's default in-process model loading for large models; models should be cached or sharded separately, or you'll OOM on instance startup.
Ignoring model format compatibility—BentoML expects standard serialization (pickle, ONNX, safetensors); custom binary formats require custom runners.
when_use: Use BentoML when you need fast model-to-production iteration, multiple models with different frameworks, or dynamic batching without managing Triton config.
when_avoid: Avoid when you need extreme latency predictability (millisecond SLAs), real-time streaming inference at petabyte scale, or you're already invested in Triton Inference Server.

faq.q: What's the difference between BentoML and Triton?
faq.a: BentoML is Python-first and ships with deployment tooling; Triton is framework-agnostic and lower-level, optimized for extreme throughput. BentoML fits teams wanting quick iteration; Triton suits high-scale inference farms.

faq.q: Can BentoML handle real-time streaming?
faq.a: BentoML supports request/response and gRPC, not streaming APIs natively. For real-time inference pipelines, pair it with a message queue (Kafka, Redis) or use Ray Serve.

faq.q: How do I deploy a BentoML service to AWS?
faq.a: Push to BentoCloud (managed), or build the bento into a Docker image and deploy to ECS/EKS manually. Yatai can also orchestrate Kubernetes clusters across clouds.

faq.q: Does BentoML support multi-model serving?
faq.a: Yes—define multiple methods in your service class, each with its own model. BentoML routes requests to the right method; you handle load-balancing between models.

faq.q: What frameworks does BentoML support?
faq.a: PyTorch, TensorFlow, scikit-learn, XGBoost, ONNX, custom Python callables. Any framework that serializes to disk is fair game.

title: BitsAndBytes
tldr: Library that shrinks LLM memory footprint via 8-bit and 4-bit quantization, letting larger models run on smaller GPUs.
plain_english: BitsAndBytes compresses a model's weights down to 8 bits or 4 bits before you load it onto your GPU. Think of it like converting a high-resolution image to lower quality to fit on a smaller disk—the model stays functional, but uses far less memory. You point Transformers at it with a config flag, and it handles the conversion automatically.
how_it_works: BitsAndBytes quantizes weights to 8-bit (LLM.int8) or 4-bit (NF4/FP4 formats) at load time via HuggingFace Transformers integration. Set `load_in_8bit=True` or pass a BitsAndBytesConfig object; the library scales activations dynamically during inference to preserve numerical stability. 4-bit mode adds optional double-quantization to compress the scale factors themselves, cutting memory again. Computation happens in higher precision (mixed-precision), so quality loss stays minimal across most tasks.
why_it_matters: It's the easiest production path to running a 70B+ parameter model on consumer GPUs. Fine-tuning becomes accessible via QLoRA without buying enterprise hardware. Inference latency on memory-bound workloads often improves because you're moving less data around.
example: A researcher fine-tunes Llama 2 70B on a single RTX 4090 using BitsAndBytes 4-bit + QLoRA. Normally, loading the full 70B model requires ~140 GB VRAM; BitsAndBytes cuts it to ~20 GB, leaving room for gradients. The resulting model quality is indistinguishable from full-precision fine-tuning on standard benchmarks.
pitfalls: 8-bit mode's dynamic quantization adds 15–25% inference latency vs. native fp16; use it for throughput-unconstrained contexts.
4-bit NF4 assumes data is normally distributed; outlier-heavy weight distributions lose more precision than typical.
Quantization is baked at load time; you cannot toggle precision per layer or swap scales dynamically during inference.
BitsAndBytes relies on NVIDIA CUDA; AMD and CPU inference require workarounds like GPTQ or AWQ.
when_use: Use BitsAndBytes when you need to fit a large model on constrained VRAM, plan QLoRA fine-tuning, or want the simplest Transformers integration for quantization.
when_avoid: Avoid it if you require sub-millisecond latency, need on-device mixed-precision tuning, or must support non-NVIDIA accelerators natively.

faq.q: Does BitsAndBytes work with any model?
faq.a: It works with Transformers-compatible models. Some custom architectures or older checkpoint formats may not load cleanly. Always test on your specific model before deployment.

faq.q: How much faster is inference with BitsAndBytes quantization?
faq.a: Memory bandwidth improves significantly, but 8-bit adds ~15–25% latency overhead. The net gain depends on your batch size and memory bandwidth vs. compute ratio; smaller batches on memory-bound hardware see the biggest win.

faq.q: Is 4-bit quantization worse quality than 8-bit?
faq.a: On average, no. NF4 (normalized float 4-bit) is optimized for LLM weight distributions and typically matches or exceeds 8-bit quality. Trade-off is memory (4-bit is half the size) vs. minor numerical precision in edge cases.

faq.q: Can I use BitsAndBytes for training or only inference?
faq.a: BitsAndBytes quantizes at load time for inference and QLoRA fine-tuning. Full training in lower precision requires separate tools like bfloat16 or mixed-precision via PyTorch.

faq.q: What GPUs does BitsAndBytes support?
faq.a: NVIDIA GPUs (compute capability 7.0+). It does not natively support AMD or Intel GPUs; alternatives like GPTQ or AWQ may work for those platforms.

title: Chunked Prefill
tldr: Splitting long input processing into small chunks to let other requests proceed, cutting tail latency.
plain_english: When your model processes a long document (like a 30k-token RAG context), it locks the GPU for several seconds. Chunked prefill breaks that work into small 512-token chunks and interleaves them with other requests' decode work. The result: other users don't wait as long, and you don't lose overall throughput.
how_it_works: The GPU normally processes an entire prefill (input tokens) before starting decoding (output generation) for any request. With chunked prefill, you split the prefill into chunks—say, 512 tokens each—and process them one at a time. Between chunks, the scheduler runs decode steps for other requests in the batch. After all prefill chunks finish, that request enters normal decoding. This interleaving lets shorter requests get their output tokens sooner, even while long-context requests are still being prepared.
why_it_matters: Multi-tenant serving mixes short chat requests with long-context tasks (RAG, analysis, summarization). Without chunking, a single 30k-token prefill delays everyone else's next decode step by 2–5 seconds. Chunked prefill keeps tail latency low for short requests while keeping the long-context request throughput almost identical, making your system feel more responsive under mixed load.
example: A RAG service handles both quick Q&A (100 tokens) and document analysis (50k tokens). Without chunking, a user asking 'What is X?' waits 5+ seconds while a document request's prefill runs. With chunked prefill (512-token chunks), the Q&A request gets its first output token in 200ms because decode work interleaves between prefill chunks. The document request finishes in roughly the same total time, but everyone else benefits.
pitfalls: Chunking adds context-switch overhead; too-small chunks (e.g., 64 tokens) hurt throughput more than they help latency—balance is critical.
Requests with very large batch sizes can still block if the scheduler doesn't prioritize decoding; chunking alone doesn't guarantee fairness.
Some hardware (older GPUs, high-latency interconnects) sees worse performance because chunk switching has setup costs that dominate on small batches.
Not all frameworks implement it; vLLM and SGLang support it, but older inference servers may not, locking you into specific infrastructure choices.
when_use: Use chunked prefill when you serve mixed workloads (short and long inputs), tail latency matters more than raw throughput, and your batches include at least a few concurrent requests.
when_avoid: Avoid chunked prefill if you only serve long-context requests with small batch sizes, latency is not a bottleneck, or your hardware has very high context-switch overhead.

faq.q: Does chunked prefill reduce throughput?
faq.a: Slightly, because context switching adds overhead. Well-tuned chunk sizes (256–1024 tokens) lose <5% throughput but cut tail latency by 50%+. Poor tuning can hurt both.

faq.q: How is chunked prefill different from continuous batching?
faq.a: Continuous batching schedules new requests between decode steps of active ones. Chunked prefill does the same thing but *within* a single request's prefill phase, interleaving its work with other requests' decodes.

faq.q: What chunk size should I use?
faq.a: Start with 512 tokens and measure latency vs. throughput. Smaller chunks (128–256) favor latency; larger chunks (1024–2048) favor throughput. Your optimal size depends on hardware, batch size, and workload mix.

faq.q: Can I use chunked prefill with speculative decoding?
faq.a: Yes, they're orthogonal. Speculative decoding drafts multiple tokens at once; chunked prefill schedules prefill chunks. Both can run together, but tuning becomes more complex.

faq.q: Do I need to change my model or code to use chunked prefill?
faq.a: No. It's an inference server feature (vLLM, SGLang) that you enable via configuration, not a model-level change.

title: Sandbox (LLM Code Execution)
tldr: Isolated runtime environment where LLM-generated code executes without access to the host system.
plain_english: A sandbox is a locked container where you safely run code that an LLM wrote. Think of it like a virtual machine with the walls cranked up—no network access by default, no reading arbitrary files, no escape hatches. You use it whenever an agent needs to execute code and you don't want that code to delete your database or call home.
how_it_works: When an LLM outputs code, you send it to a sandbox instead of running it locally. The sandbox provider (E2B, Riza, Modal, or custom Docker) spins up an isolated environment with CPU/RAM limits, a restricted filesystem, and blocked network access. Your code runs inside. If it tries to escape or exceed limits, the isolation layer stops it. Results come back to your application. When done, the sandbox terminates and leaves no trace.
why_it_matters: Running untrusted code is a security liability and a product trust issue. A sandbox turns "LLM wrote some code" from "oh no" into "okay, we can ship this." It's the difference between shipping an agent and shipping a liability.
example: An AI code assistant at Replit lets users ask it to write scripts. Instead of executing code in the user's browser or server (where it could read API keys or other users' files), Replit routes it through a Firecracker microVM sandbox. The code runs isolated, times out after 30 seconds, and only returns stdout. If the code tries to make network requests or access the filesystem outside its allocation, the sandbox kernel blocks it.
pitfalls: Assuming network isolation alone is enough: you still need filesystem and process limits, or a malicious loop burns resources and blocks other users.
Not setting hard timeouts: infinite loops or blocked I/O can hang your sandbox, wasting resources and timing out legitimate requests.
Persisting state between runs: if your sandbox caches libraries or data, one user's code can leak information to the next.
Choosing the wrong abstraction: Docker has larger overhead; Firecracker is faster but harder to orchestrate; WebAssembly is lightweight but language-limited.
when_use: Use a sandbox when an LLM or user input controls code execution—agents running tools, code interpreters, REPL interfaces, or automated script generation.
when_avoid: Avoid if the code is pre-vetted and static (no LLM generation) or if the latency overhead (100–500ms spin-up) breaks your SLA.

faq.q: What's the difference between a sandbox and a container?
faq.a: A container (Docker) isolates via filesystem and process namespaces; a sandbox adds kernel-level isolation (Firecracker) or language-level isolation (WebAssembly). Sandboxes are stricter and slower to start but prevent more classes of escape.

faq.q: Can code in a sandbox access my files or database?
faq.a: Only if you explicitly grant access via mounted volumes or environment variables. By default, no—the sandbox has an empty or read-only filesystem and no network.

faq.q: How long does a sandbox take to start up?
faq.a: Docker: 500ms–2s. Firecracker: 100–300ms. WebAssembly: <50ms. E2B and Modal manage this for you, pooling warm instances to hide latency.

faq.q: What happens if code runs forever or crashes in a sandbox?
faq.a: The sandbox enforces a hard timeout (configurable, often 30–60s) and kills the process. Crashes are caught and returned as error output, not crashing your host.

faq.q: Do I need a sandbox if I only run code from my own team?
faq.a: Not strictly, but you do if the code is generated by an LLM or user input. For internal, pre-written scripts, the security win is smaller—focus on testing and code review instead.

DefinedTerm.description: Isolated runtime environment where LLM-generated code executes without access to the host system.

mainEntity.name: What's the difference between a sandbox and a container?

acceptedAnswer.text: A container (Docker) isolates via filesystem and process namespaces; a sandbox adds kernel-level isolation (Firecracker) or language-level isolation (WebAssembly). Sandboxes are stricter and slower to start but prevent more classes of escape.

mainEntity.name: Can code in a sandbox access my files or database?

acceptedAnswer.text: Only if you explicitly grant access via mounted volumes or environment variables. By default, no—the sandbox has an empty or read-only filesystem and no network.

mainEntity.name: How long does a sandbox take to start up?

acceptedAnswer.text: Docker: 500ms–2s. Firecracker: 100–300ms. WebAssembly: <50ms. E2B and Modal manage this for you, pooling warm instances to hide latency.

mainEntity.name: What happens if code runs forever or crashes in a sandbox?

acceptedAnswer.text: The sandbox enforces a hard timeout (configurable, often 30–60s) and kills the process. Crashes are caught and returned as error output, not crashing your host.

mainEntity.name: Do I need a sandbox if I only run code from my own team?

acceptedAnswer.text: Not strictly, but you do if the code is generated by an LLM or user input. For internal, pre-written scripts, the security win is smaller—focus on testing and code review instead.

title: Cog (Replicate)
tldr: Open-source tool that packages Python ML models into production Docker containers with typed predict functions.
plain_english: Cog lets you wrap a Python ML model in a container without writing Dockerfile boilerplate. You define your model's inputs and outputs as typed Python functions, and Cog handles the rest—building a Docker image that runs anywhere. Think of it as a mold that turns messy research code into a standardized, deployable artifact.
how_it_works: You create two files: cog.yaml (runtime config—Python version, GPU, dependencies) and predict.py (a class with a predict() method that takes typed inputs and returns outputs). Cog reads these, wraps them into a Dockerfile, and builds a container. The container exposes an HTTP API automatically. You run it locally, push to Replicate, or deploy to Kubernetes—same image everywhere. Input types use Pydantic-like schemas for validation.
why_it_matters: Shipping ML models to production usually means wrestling with Dockerfiles, environment management, and API frameworks. Cog abstracts those layers so your team ships models in hours, not days. It's especially useful if you iterate between local experimentation and cloud deployment, since the same cog.yaml works everywhere.
example: A computer-vision team at a startup trains a YOLO model and wants to expose it as an API. They write a predict.py that loads the model and takes an image URL as input, returning bounding boxes. One cog.yaml pins PyTorch version and requests an A100 GPU. Running `cog build` produces a container; pushing it to Replicate takes two CLI commands. Within an hour, the API is live and autoscales.
pitfalls: GPU dependencies in cog.yaml (cuda, pytorch) can bloat images; test locally with Docker first to catch silent failures.
Predict function signatures must match Pydantic field definitions exactly, or the API will reject valid-looking requests.
Cog caches Docker layers aggressively; code changes may not rebuild unless you clear the cache or bump a dependency version.
Cold starts in serverless environments (including Replicate) can exceed 30 seconds if the model is large; prefetch weights in __init__.
when_use: Use Cog when you have a Python ML model, want reproducible containerization, and deploy to Replicate, Kubernetes, or any Docker-compatible infrastructure.
when_avoid: Avoid Cog if you need custom networking, multi-service orchestration, or don't use Python; use raw Docker or Kubernetes manifests instead.

faq.q: Is Cog the same as Docker?
faq.a: No. Cog generates Dockerfiles for you based on a simple config; Docker is the underlying container runtime. Cog saves boilerplate for ML-specific patterns.

faq.q: Can I use Cog outside Replicate?
faq.a: Yes. Cog builds standard Docker images. Once built, deploy to Kubernetes, cloud VMs, or any Docker host—Replicate is optional.

faq.q: How do I add GPU support to my Cog model?
faq.a: Add `gpu: true` and optionally `gpu_count` to cog.yaml. Cog will bake CUDA into the image and the container will request GPU at runtime.

faq.q: What happens if my model is too large for the Docker image?
faq.a: Use a base image with preinstalled weights, or download weights at runtime inside predict(). Cog supports custom base images via the `base_image` field in cog.yaml.

faq.q: Do I need to know Docker to use Cog?
faq.a: No. Cog hides Docker details. You write Python and YAML; Cog handles image creation. Understanding Docker basics helps debug, but is not required to start.

title: Context Length Extension
tldr: Post-training techniques that extend a model's usable context window beyond training length via position encoding scaling and fine-tuning.
plain_english: Models trained on short sequences (e.g., 4k tokens) degrade in quality when you feed them longer documents. Context extension is like teaching a model to handle longer documents by adjusting how it counts position—think of it as recalibrating its internal ruler—then practicing on real long documents. The result: a model that handles 64k or 128k tokens with minimal quality loss.
how_it_works: Extension combines three steps: (1) rescale the rotary position embedding (RoPE) frequencies using methods like YaRN or NTK-aware scaling to extrapolate beyond the training range, (2) fine-tune on actual long-sequence data (typically 1–10B tokens) with the rescaled encoding active, (3) optionally add sliding-window or streaming attention to the longer-context layers for efficiency. The rescaling adjusts the frequency base so position indices don't saturate; fine-tuning teaches the model to trust those new positions. Most open-source long-context models (Llama 2 70B extended to 100k, Mistral 7B to 200k) used this recipe.
why_it_matters: Context extension is the most practical way to unlock long-context capability in existing models without retraining from scratch. It clarifies the quality trade-offs: you gain 16–64× more context window but incur fine-tuning cost and a small accuracy penalty on the original training distribution. Understanding the mechanics helps you choose between extension, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), or buying a natively long-context model.
example: A team fine-tunes Llama 2 7B (originally 4k context) using YaRN scaling + 2B tokens of bookshelf text and code to reach 32k tokens. They spend ~1 week of compute (e.g., 8×A100s) and validate on the LongBench benchmark. The extended model handles full legal documents and codebases in a single prompt but shows 2–3% perplexity increase on the original 4k MMLU benchmark compared to the base model.
pitfalls: Rescaling alone without fine-tuning produces garbage outputs beyond training length; the model has never seen those position indices.
Fine-tuning on short documents with rescaled encodings does not generalize; you must train on actual long sequences.
Underestimating the compute cost: 1–10B tokens of fine-tuning on long sequences is expensive; factor in full-precision or mixed-precision overhead.
Ignoring the lost-in-the-middle effect: models still struggle to extract signals from the middle of long contexts after extension, especially on retrieval tasks.
when_use: Use context extension when you own the base model, have a clear long-context use case (e.g., document QA, code analysis), can afford 1–2 weeks of GPU time, and need a bespoke model rather than an API.
when_avoid: Avoid if you need native long-context performance on specialized tasks (use a purpose-built model instead), lack access to long-sequence training data, or can solve the problem with RAG or prompt engineering.

faq.q: Can I extend context length without fine-tuning?
faq.a: No. Rescaling RoPE alone allows the model to accept longer inputs, but outputs will be nonsensical or hallucinated. Fine-tuning is required to teach the model to trust and use the rescaled positions.

faq.q: How much data do I need to fine-tune for context extension?
faq.a: Typically 1–10B tokens of actual long sequences. Smaller datasets (100M–500M) work for incremental extension (4k→8k) but yield poor results for large jumps (4k→128k).

faq.q: Does context extension hurt model quality on short prompts?
faq.a: Slightly. You usually see a 1–5% perplexity increase on the original training distribution. The model trades off short-context precision for long-context generalization.

faq.q: What is the difference between YaRN and NTK-aware scaling?
faq.a: YaRN applies a non-linear interpolation to RoPE frequencies and uses per-head scaling; NTK-aware is simpler, using a single base-frequency adjustment. YaRN typically yields better long-context quality but is more complex to implement.

faq.q: Can I extend a quantized model (GPTQ, AWQ)?
faq.a: It is difficult. You need to dequantize, fine-tune, then re-quantize, which defeats much of the memory benefit. Most practitioners extend before quantization or use tools like Unsloth to reduce fine-tuning overhead.

title: Context Window
tldr: The maximum number of tokens an AI model can read and reference in a single request.
plain_english: Think of context window as an AI's working memory during a single conversation or task. If you paste a 50-page document but the model's context window is only 8,000 tokens (roughly 6,000 words), it can only see part of it. Exceed the limit and earlier content gets dropped—the model forgets what you told it at the start.
how_it_works: Every token (roughly one word or punctuation mark) in your input message counts against the context window. The model processes all tokens up to the limit, then stops accepting new input. Output tokens also consume window space. If you have a 4K context window and send 3,500 tokens of input, you have ~500 tokens left for the model's response. Longer context windows (like Claude 3.5 Sonnet's 200K) let you fit entire codebases, books, or conversation histories in one request without truncation.
why_it_matters: Context window determines whether you can work with full documents in one go or need to split them into chunks. Smaller windows force you into workarounds—retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), chunking, or multiple API calls—adding complexity and latency. Larger windows simplify workflows and let the model reason across broader scope, but they cost more tokens per request.
example: A code review tool at a startup wants to analyze a 100KB codebase at once. GPT-4 Turbo's 128K context window handles it in a single request. A team using a smaller 4K-window model must split the code into 20 requests, each requiring separate API overhead and context re-establishment. The larger window saves engineering time and API costs, but Claude's 200K option costs more per token—tradeoff depends on your document size and query volume.
pitfalls: Context isn't free: longer windows consume more tokens even if you don't use them all—you pay for the full input regardless.
Lost-in-the-middle: models often ignore information buried in the middle of long contexts; place critical details at the start or end.
Token counting varies by model; GPT-4 and Claude tokenize differently, so the same text may use more tokens in one model than another.
Confusing context window with max output length: a 128K window means 128K tokens total input+output, not 128K output.
when_use: Use a large context window when you need to analyze full documents, long conversations, or code in one request, or when you want to avoid engineering RAG pipelines.
when_avoid: Avoid relying on huge context windows for production APIs if cost is tight; consider RAG or chunking for repeated queries over the same large corpus.

faq.q: What is the difference between context window and max output length?
faq.a: Context window is your total token budget for input plus output combined. Max output length (or max tokens parameter) caps only the response. A 128K context window with max_tokens=4096 means 128K total, with at most 4K used for the answer.

faq.q: How do I know if my text fits in the context window?
faq.a: Use the model's tokenizer to count tokens before sending (e.g., tiktoken for OpenAI, Claude's token counter). Roughly: 1 token ≈ 0.75 words in English. Always leave headroom for the response.

faq.q: Why does my model give bad answers for long documents?
faq.a: Lost-in-the-middle effect: models often skip or misweight information in the middle. Restructure critical details to the start or end, or use RAG to retrieve only relevant passages instead of dumping entire documents.

faq.q: Is a larger context window always better?
faq.a: No. Larger windows cost more per token, introduce latency, and don't guarantee better answers for unfocused queries. RAG or chunking is often cheaper and faster for repeated searches over large datasets.

faq.q: Can I extend a model's context window?
faq.a: Some models support techniques like RoPE scaling or fine-tuning on long sequences, but you can't arbitrarily extend beyond what the model was trained for without performance loss. Using a longer-context model is simpler.

title: Continuous Batching (Inflight Batching)
tldr: LLM serving pattern where new requests join in-progress batches at each decode step, boosting GPU utilization 2–5x.
plain_english: Imagine a checkout line where customers can jump in after each person pays, instead of waiting for a full group before processing starts. Continuous batching works the same way: your LLM server admits new inference requests into the active batch every decoding step, while finished ones exit. Slower requests no longer block faster ones, so your GPU stays busy on useful work instead of idle padding.
how_it_works: Traditional batching fills a batch, decodes all sequences in lockstep until the longest finishes, then starts a new batch. Continuous batching uses iteration-level scheduling: at each decode step, it evicts completed sequences and packs new requests into the freed slots. The batch size stays constant but the sequence IDs rotate. Each new request can start immediately—it enters the next available slot rather than waiting for a batch-wide completion. This requires dynamic memory management (like paged attention) to avoid fragmentation.
why_it_matters: Mixed-workload inference traffic includes both short and long completions. Without continuous batching, a single long request holds GPU resources until done, starving short requests. With it, you see 2–5x higher throughput on real traffic and lower tail latency, making serving cost-efficient at scale. This is the standard pattern in vLLM, TensorRT-LLM, and modern production stacks.
example: A chatbot deployment serving 1000 concurrent users receives requests for summaries (10 tokens), follow-up questions (50 tokens), and code generation (500 tokens). Static batching with batch size 32 forces the 500-token request to monopolize a GPU slot for 500 steps. With continuous batching, that slot re-fills with 50 new summary requests by step 50, boosting utilization from ~30% to ~85% on typical workloads.
pitfalls: Fragmented KV-cache: aggressive iteration-level eviction can scatter allocations across memory, reducing cache hit rates unless you use paged attention or prefix caching.
Scheduling complexity: poorly tuned scheduling policies (greedy vs. longest-first) can create tail latency spikes by starving long sequences with low priority weights.
Padding overhead at the boundary: if new requests arrive slower than old ones complete, you may still have unused batch slots and wasted compute.
Visibility into tokenization latency: continuous batching hides per-token cost variance, making it harder to debug whether slowness is tokenization, attention, or I/O.
when_use: Use continuous batching for any production LLM endpoint serving multiple users or a mix of request lengths; it's the default standard in high-throughput inference.
when_avoid: Avoid if you have strict request-isolation requirements, single-request-at-a-time serving, or if your hardware (old GPU, small VRAM) cannot support paged attention or dynamic memory management.

faq.q: How is continuous batching different from dynamic batching?
faq.a: Dynamic batching waits for multiple requests to arrive, then processes them together. Continuous batching admits requests mid-batch at each decode step. Continuous is faster for mixed-length workloads because it doesn't force short requests to wait for a full batch to form.

faq.q: Do I need paged attention to use continuous batching?
faq.a: No, but it's strongly recommended. Without it, moving sequences in and out of batch slots fragments KV-cache memory. Paged attention (like in vLLM) treats cache as virtual blocks, making continuous batching safe and efficient.

faq.q: Will continuous batching reduce latency for my requests?
faq.a: It improves time-to-first-token and throughput overall, but latency for a single long request may stay the same. Short requests see much lower latency because they don't wait for long ones to complete before entering the queue.

faq.q: What batch size should I use with continuous batching?
faq.a: Choose the largest batch size that fits in VRAM (usually 32–512 depending on model size and GPU). Continuous batching keeps the batch full dynamically, so you don't need to tune it per-workload like static batching.

faq.q: Does continuous batching work with multi-GPU or tensor parallelism?
faq.a: Yes. Implementations like vLLM handle it transparently across distributed setups. Scheduling decisions happen on the coordinator, and KV-cache partitions follow the parallelism strategy.

title: CoreML (Apple)
tldr: Apple's on-device ML framework that runs models on iOS and macOS via Neural Engine, GPU, or CPU.
plain_english: CoreML is Apple's runtime for running trained machine learning models directly on iPhones, iPads, and Macs without sending data to a server. Think of it as a translator that takes a model you trained elsewhere (PyTorch, TensorFlow) and converts it into a format Apple devices understand, then automatically picks the fastest hardware path—whether that's a specialized chip, graphics processor, or main CPU.
how_it_works: CoreML Tools convert PyTorch and TensorFlow models to the .mlpackage format. At runtime, CoreML's compiler selects the best backend: the Apple Neural Engine (a specialized accelerator in modern chips), Metal Performance Shaders (GPU), or the main CPU. The framework handles memory layout, quantization, and caching automatically. Model inference happens on-device with no network round-trip, keeping latency low and data private.
why_it_matters: For iOS and macOS apps, CoreML is the only native on-device ML platform. Running models locally means faster response times, offline capability, and no telemetry—critical for productivity apps, AR experiences, and privacy-sensitive use cases. Small LLMs (7B parameters, quantized) run acceptably on M-series Macs via CoreML or MLX.
example: A photo editing app on iPhone uses CoreML to run an image segmentation model that separates foreground from background in real-time as the user frames a shot. The model runs on the Neural Engine; the app gets results in 50ms without uploading the photo anywhere. Apple's own Apple Intelligence features (2024) rely on CoreML for on-device text processing and summarization.
pitfalls: Assuming CoreML automatically optimizes all models; you still need quantization and pruning for mobile latency targets.
Forgetting that conversion to .mlpackage can fail or produce precision loss—always validate outputs against the original.
Treating on-device as 'free'—inference still consumes battery and thermal budget; profile on real hardware.
Expecting CoreML to auto-select GPU over Neural Engine; the compiler heuristics are opaque and platform-dependent.
when_use: Use CoreML when you need on-device inference on iOS/macOS, prioritize privacy and latency, or must work offline.
when_avoid: Avoid CoreML if your model is large (>1GB), requires frequent retraining, or your team lacks iOS expertise for integration.

faq.q: What's the difference between CoreML and MLX?
faq.a: CoreML is Apple's on-device inference framework for apps; MLX is a research library for training and finetuning on Apple Silicon Macs. They're complementary—train on MLX, deploy with CoreML.

faq.q: Can I run large language models with CoreML?
faq.a: Yes, but only small quantized LLMs (7B parameters or fewer). Models larger than ~3B tokens per second become impractical on mobile due to memory and latency constraints.

faq.q: Does CoreML require internet connection?
faq.a: No. CoreML runs inference entirely on-device. The model is embedded in your app binary or downloaded once; inference needs no network.

faq.q: How do I convert my PyTorch model to CoreML?
faq.a: Use coreml-tools: export to ONNX or intermediate format, then call ct.convert() with your model. Check the output type and test on real hardware.

faq.q: Will CoreML use the Neural Engine automatically?
faq.a: CoreML's compiler tries to map operations to the Neural Engine, but success depends on op support and model structure. Profile to confirm GPU or Neural Engine usage.

title: CPU Offloading
tldr: Move model weights or optimizer state to CPU RAM, stream to GPU as needed, to fit larger models on limited VRAM.
plain_english: CPU offloading is like extending your GPU's memory by using your system RAM as a spillover. Instead of everything living on the GPU at once, you keep some weights in CPU RAM and fetch them over PCIe when the model needs them. You trade speed for capacity: PCIe is much slower than GPU memory bandwidth, so inference or training gets slower, but you can now run models that physically wouldn't fit.
how_it_works: During training or inference, the model partitions weights, gradients, or optimizer state across CPU and GPU. When forward or backward pass needs a weight chunk, it's fetched from CPU RAM over PCIe, used, then discarded or written back. DeepSpeed ZeRO-Infinity, FSDP with CPU-offload, and bitsandbytes all implement this. The scheduler decides which tensors stay GPU-resident (hot path) and which live CPU-side (cold path). PCIe bandwidth (typically 16 GB/s per direction) is the bottleneck; GPU memory bandwidth is 100–900 GB/s, so latency compounds with model size.
why_it_matters: CPU offloading is the last resort to train or run models that physically exceed GPU VRAM. For teams with fixed hardware (e.g., 2× H100 with 80 GB each), it means fitting a 200B-parameter model instead of being blocked. The tradeoff is real: throughput often drops 40–70%, so it's not a shortcut for production serving. It buys you the ability to experiment or fine-tune on constrained budgets.
example: A research team with four A100 40GB GPUs (160 GB total VRAM) wants to fine-tune LLaMA 2 70B with full precision (280 GB for weights alone). Without offloading, it's impossible. With FSDP CPU-offload enabled, they keep only the active layers on GPU and stream non-active weights from system RAM. Training runs at 15 tokens/sec instead of 50 tokens/sec, but they complete their experiment in 48 hours instead of waiting for cloud credits.
pitfalls: PCIe saturation under high batch sizes: offloading overhead compounds; large batches may actually slow down further.
Uneven offloading across devices: if only some parameters are offloaded, GPU-CPU bandwidth becomes the bottleneck even for GPU-resident tensors.
Optimizer state explosion: Adam and other adaptive optimizers store multiple buffers per parameter; offloading state separately from weights can fragment memory and hurt PCIe efficiency.
Ignoring NVLink or multi-GPU topology: if you have NVLink-connected GPUs, tensor parallelism often beats CPU offloading for larger models.
when_use: Use CPU offloading when VRAM is your hard constraint, you have no alternative (more GPUs, smaller models, quantization alone won't fit), and you can accept 40–70% throughput loss for the ability to train or run at all.
when_avoid: Avoid CPU offloading if your model already fits in VRAM, you need maximum throughput for production serving, you have high-latency or low-bandwidth PCIe (e.g., older systems), or you can solve the problem with quantization or distillation instead.

faq.q: How much slower is CPU offloading than keeping everything on GPU?
faq.a: Typically 40–70% throughput loss, depending on model size, batch size, and PCIe bandwidth. Smaller batches and larger models see worse slowdown because CPU–GPU communication overhead becomes a higher fraction of total time.

faq.q: Is CPU offloading the same as disk offloading?
faq.a: No. CPU offloading uses system RAM (latency ~100 ns, bandwidth ~50 GB/s). Disk offloading uses NVMe or SSDs (latency ~10 μs, bandwidth ~1–7 GB/s), which is 100× slower and rarely practical for training. Disk offloading appears in some inference systems for rarely-used weights.

faq.q: Can I use CPU offloading with quantization to fit even bigger models?
faq.a: Yes. Quantization reduces parameter size (e.g., FP8 is 1/2 the size of FP16), so you have more room on GPU before offloading. DeepSpeed and bitsandbytes both support both techniques. Combining them extends capacity further but adds computational overhead from dequantization.

faq.q: Does CPU offloading work with multi-GPU training?
faq.a: Yes, but it depends on the framework. FSDP CPU-offload works across distributed setups; each rank offloads to its local CPU. DeepSpeed ZeRO-Infinity also supports it. However, you still need sufficient CPU RAM per node—distributed offloading doesn't magically give you one global CPU pool.

faq.q: What's the minimum CPU RAM needed for CPU offloading?
faq.a: At least as much as the model's parameter size in your target dtype (e.g., 280 GB for 70B params in FP32). In practice, add headroom for optimizer state and activations; 1.5–2× the parameter size is a safe estimate. Swap-to-disk will destroy performance, so don't rely on it.

title: CrewAI
tldr: Python framework for multi-agent teams with defined roles, goals, and collaboration processes.
plain_english: CrewAI treats AI agents like team members: you give each a role (e.g., researcher, writer), a goal, and backstory. They work together on tasks through sequential or hierarchical workflows. Think of it as staffing an actual department where each agent knows its job and how to work with others.
how_it_works: You define agents with role, goal, backstory, and assigned tools. Group agents into a crew and specify tasks they execute. CrewAI runs tasks through defined processes—sequential (one after another), hierarchical (manager delegates), or custom flows. Agents exchange outputs, maintaining memory of prior steps. The framework handles task routing, tool calls, and inter-agent communication.
why_it_matters: CrewAI's persona-driven structure makes multi-agent workflows intuitive and maintainable for vertical use cases (marketing teams, research squads, content pipelines). It strikes a middle ground between rigid task orchestration and blank-slate frameworks, reducing boilerplate for team-like behaviors.
example: A marketing agency runs a content team: SEO researcher agent gathers competitor data, writer agent drafts the post, editor agent reviews it. CrewAI executes these sequentially, passing outputs forward. Without it, you'd manually parse tool outputs and route them between models.
pitfalls: Agent personas are cosmetic if not reflected in prompts and tool access—a 'researcher' with no retrieval tools won't act like one.
Sequential processes become bottlenecks; hierarchical delegation requires careful goal alignment or agents deadlock waiting for unclear decisions.
Memory between tasks is not persistent by default; you must explicitly design context passing or use external memory layers.
Scaling beyond ~5 agents gets expensive and slow; token costs multiply with inter-agent communication overhead.
when_use: Use CrewAI when you need multi-agent workflows with clear roles (research → writing → editing), personas enhance task clarity, or you're building vertical demos like internal tool squads.
when_avoid: Avoid CrewAI for single-agent tasks, purely data-parallel work (use orchestration instead), or when agents need to act autonomously without fixed roles or processes.

faq.q: Is CrewAI the same as AutoGen?
faq.a: No. CrewAI emphasizes persona-driven agents and defined team workflows; AutoGen focuses on agent communication patterns and conversation dynamics. CrewAI is more opinionated about structure; AutoGen is more flexible for research and custom architectures.

faq.q: How does CrewAI compare to LangGraph?
faq.a: LangGraph is a graph-based workflow builder without built-in agent concepts; CrewAI wraps agents with roles and backstories. LangGraph gives you lower-level control; CrewAI trades that for faster team-oriented setup.

faq.q: Can I use CrewAI for production systems?
faq.a: Yes, but plan for observability, error handling, and cost monitoring. CrewAI agents make multiple LLM calls per task, so token costs scale quickly. Use structured outputs and external memory to keep costs predictable.

faq.q: Do I need to write custom code for agents to work together?
faq.a: Minimal. CrewAI handles task routing and output passing automatically. You define task sequence and success criteria; the framework does the plumbing. Custom logic is only needed for non-standard flows.

faq.q: What's the difference between sequential and hierarchical process?
faq.a: Sequential executes tasks one after another in order. Hierarchical appoints a manager agent that decides task order and which agents work on what, adding flexibility but complexity.

title: CUDA
tldr: Nvidia's parallel-computing platform that lets you run code on Nvidia GPUs; the de facto standard for ML workloads.
plain_english: CUDA is Nvidia's toolkit that translates C/C++ code into instructions Nvidia GPUs understand. Think of it as a language bridge: you write portable code, CUDA compiles it to run fast on Nvidia hardware. PyTorch, TensorFlow, and JAX all use CUDA under the hood—you rarely touch it directly, but it's doing the heavy lifting.
how_it_works: You write compute kernels in CUDA C/C++ (or use a framework that does it for you). The CUDA compiler (nvcc) converts that code into GPU machine instructions. Libraries like cuBLAS (matrix math), cuDNN (neural networks), and cuFFT (Fourier transforms) provide optimized routines. Your CPU launches tasks onto the GPU, the GPU executes them in parallel across thousands of cores, and results come back to CPU memory. Framework APIs hide most of this—PyTorch's `.cuda()` method is a CUDA call under the hood.
why_it_matters: CUDA dominance is why Nvidia owns the AI compute market. 15 years of tooling, libraries, and compiler maturity mean Nvidia GPUs run ML workloads faster than competitors on day one. Switching to AMD (ROCm) or Intel (oneAPI) means losing that ecosystem advantage, which is why hardware vendors spend years chasing CUDA compatibility rather than replacing it.
example: OpenAI's training infrastructure relies on CUDA across Nvidia H100 and A100 clusters. When PyTorch calls `model.cuda()`, it's delegating matrix multiplications, convolutions, and attention operations to cuDNN and cuBLAS running on those GPUs. Without CUDA's maturity and optimization, that training would be orders of magnitude slower.
pitfalls: CUDA code ties you to Nvidia hardware—porting to AMD or custom silicon requires rewriting or using abstraction layers like Triton.
GPU memory (VRAM) is separate from CPU memory; moving data back and forth creates bottlenecks if you're not careful about batch sizes and prefetching.
CUDA version mismatches between your code, frameworks, and driver often cause cryptic runtime failures; pin exact versions in containers.
Not all operations are GPU-accelerated; CPU-GPU synchronization points can silently tank throughput if you're not profiling.
when_use: Use CUDA when you're training or running inference on Nvidia GPUs and want maximum performance—standard choice for PyTorch, TensorFlow, and JAX workloads.
when_avoid: Avoid direct CUDA programming unless you're optimizing tight loops; frameworks abstract it well. Skip CUDA entirely if you're locked into AMD, Intel, or mobile hardware.

faq.q: What's the difference between CUDA and PyTorch?
faq.a: PyTorch is a framework for building models; CUDA is the low-level toolkit that makes PyTorch fast on Nvidia GPUs. PyTorch calls CUDA functions internally when you use `.cuda()` or `.to('cuda')`.

faq.q: Can I use CUDA on AMD GPUs?
faq.a: Not directly. AMD offers ROCm as their CUDA equivalent. Some frameworks (PyTorch, TensorFlow) support ROCm, but you lose CUDA's ecosystem maturity and usually see slower performance.

faq.q: Do I need to write CUDA code myself?
faq.a: Rarely. Frameworks like PyTorch handle CUDA for you. You only write custom CUDA kernels if you're optimizing a bottleneck that standard libraries don't cover well.

faq.q: What happens if my CUDA version doesn't match my GPU driver?
faq.a: You'll get runtime errors or silent compute failures. Use `nvidia-smi` to check your driver's CUDA compatibility, then install the matching CUDA Toolkit version for your frameworks.

faq.q: Is CUDA the same as Triton?
faq.a: No. Triton is a higher-level language for writing GPU kernels without CUDA's low-level details. Triton kernels compile to CUDA or other backends, so Triton sits on top of CUDA.
