title: Decode (Generation Phase)
tldr: The inference phase where tokens are generated one at a time, reusing a cached memory structure—bandwidth-limited, not compute-limited.
plain_english: During inference, an LLM generates text one token at a time. Decode is the phase after initial context processing where each new token is computed by running a tiny calculation that mostly involves reading weights from GPU memory. Think of it like repeatedly asking a library for the same heavy books instead of doing complicated math—the bottleneck is fetching, not thinking.
how_it_works: For each token, decode runs one forward pass that attends to the accumulated KV cache—a memory structure storing previous key and value computations. This pass is a small matrix multiplication: one new row of embeddings multiplied by all weights, producing one token. The weights stay constant across all passes; you read them once per token. Batching multiple sequences lets you read weights once and use them for many concurrent token predictions, amortizing that memory cost.
why_it_matters: Decode consumes most of the latency and cost in LLM inference. Every optimization targeting decode—quantization shrinking weights, speculative decoding skipping unnecessary reads, batch scheduling—directly improves real-world performance. Understanding why decode is bandwidth-bound, not compute-bound, explains why GPU memory speed often matters more than raw computing power.
example: You run Llama 7B on 8 H100 GPUs to serve 32 concurrent users. Prefill (processing their prompts) takes 50ms total. Decode generating 100 tokens takes 4 seconds—80× longer—because each of the 100 tokens must read 7B weights from HBM, reusing the same KV cache structure built during prefill. Quantizing to 8-bit cuts weight size in half, dropping decode to 2 seconds.
pitfalls: Assuming decode scales with batch size like prefill does—decode throughput saturates when weight reads hit memory limits, unlike prefill which scales nearly linearly.
Applying prefill-centric optimizations (like flash-attention) to decode without measuring actual latency—token latency, not throughput, drives user experience.
Underestimating KV cache memory: with long context and large batch, cache footprint often exceeds weight footprint, breaking decode optimization strategies.
Treating quantization as a decode-only win—decode gets the biggest speedup, but prefill usually doesn't, requiring different quant strategies per phase.
when_use: Use decode-phase terminology when diagnosing latency bottlenecks in inference, designing batching strategies, or evaluating optimizations like quantization or speculative decoding.
when_avoid: Avoid when analyzing prefill performance, training dynamics, or offline batch processing where compute, not memory bandwidth, is the constraint.

faq.q: What's the difference between prefill and decode?
faq.a: Prefill processes the entire prompt in one pass, compute-bound. Decode generates tokens one at a time, bandwidth-bound. Prefill scales with sequence length; decode scales with tokens generated.

faq.q: Why does decode take longer than prefill even though it does less work?
faq.a: Decode repeats weight reads for every token generated. Prefill amortizes reads across many tokens in one pass. With long generations, small per-token work adds up.

faq.q: How does quantization speed up decode?
faq.a: Quantization shrinks model weights (e.g., float32 → int8). Smaller weights move faster from HBM to GPU cores. Decode reads weights once per token, so weight size directly impacts latency.

faq.q: Does batching multiple users improve decode latency?
faq.a: Batching improves throughput (tokens/sec) but not individual latency. One user still waits for their token. Batching helps cost-per-token, not time-to-next-token.

faq.q: What is the KV cache and why does it matter for decode?
faq.a: The KV cache stores computed keys and values from prior tokens, avoiding recomputation. Decode reuses this cache for every new token, making decode orders of magnitude cheaper than recomputing from scratch.

title: Diffusers (Library)
tldr: Hugging Face library providing unified pipelines and schedulers for image, video, and audio diffusion models.
plain_english: Diffusers is to diffusion models what Transformers is to language models—a standardized toolkit that wraps different architectures (Stable Diffusion, FLUX, SVD) under a consistent Python API. Instead of hunting down separate implementations for each model, you import one pipeline, pass your prompt and parameters, and get your output. It handles the messy details: noise scheduling, model loading, multi-GPU coordination.
how_it_works: You instantiate a pipeline (e.g., StableDiffusionPipeline or FluxPipeline) which loads a pretrained model and scheduler. The scheduler controls the noise-removal process across timesteps—options include DPM++, Euler, DDIM, and others trading speed for quality. You call the pipeline with text prompts and generation parameters (steps, guidance scale, seed). Internally, diffusers handles tensor allocation, device placement (CPU, single GPU, multi-GPU via Accelerate), and model inference. For fine-tuning, it provides DreamBooth and LoRA utilities that integrate with training frameworks like bitsandbytes and Accelerate.
why_it_matters: Without diffusers, each diffusion model release ships its own reference code, creating fragmentation and duplicated effort. By standardizing on diffusers, you can swap models, schedulers, and optimization techniques without rewriting inference logic. Most production deployments and research papers use diffusers as their baseline, making it the lingua franca for diffusion work in Python.
example: A team building an AI image editor can load Stable Diffusion XL, FLUX, and Sora-style video generation all through diffusers pipelines with minimal code changes. They use Accelerate to shard inference across two H100s, then add DreamBooth LoRA to fine-tune on brand-specific aesthetics. Six months later, a new model drops; they swap the model name and two lines change.
pitfalls: Assume scheduler choice doesn't matter—DPM++ needs more steps than DDIM for quality, and picking wrong trades latency for artifacts.
Run inference on CPU expecting GPU speed; diffusers doesn't auto-optimize; you must explicitly pass device and use Accelerate for multi-GPU.
Load full 7B model in float32 when you need 2-bit quantization; diffusers supports it but requires explicit enable_attention_slicing() and manual dtype casting.
Fine-tune LoRA weights without validating on held-out prompts; mode collapse on training data is common and diffusers makes it easy to miss.
when_use: Use diffusers when you're building production image/video generation, need to swap between models or schedulers, or want reference implementations for diffusion research.
when_avoid: Avoid diffusers if you need sub-millisecond latency (compile to ONNX/TensorRT instead) or are targeting non-Python deployment (use ONNX Runtime or core C++ inference engines).

faq.q: Can I use diffusers for video generation like Sora?
faq.a: Yes. Diffusers supports video diffusion models (SVD, Sora-style) through specialized pipelines. Setup is identical to image—load the pipeline, pass video/image prompts, get frames back.

faq.q: Is diffusers only for Hugging Face models?
faq.a: No. While Hugging Face hosts most reference implementations, diffusers is model-agnostic. You can wrap any diffusion architecture (trained elsewhere) into a custom pipeline.

faq.q: How do I speed up inference with diffusers?
faq.a: Use enable_attention_slicing() for memory, enable_xformers_memory_efficient_attention() for speed, reduce inference steps, or enable_ip_adapter() for LoRA merging. For multi-GPU, pass Accelerate's device_map.

faq.q: What's the difference between diffusers and Stability AI's reference code?
faq.a: Diffusers is generic and unified across models; Stability's reference is model-specific. Diffusers handles versioning, scheduler swaps, and integration with other HF tools (PEFT, Accelerate).

faq.q: Do I need CUDA to run diffusers?
faq.a: No, but GPU is strongly recommended. CPU inference is 50–100× slower. Diffusers supports CUDA, ROCm, and Metal (Apple Silicon). MPS support is partial.

title: Disk Offloading (NVMe Offload)
tldr: Spilling optimizer state and model parameters to NVMe SSDs when GPU/CPU memory fills, trading speed for capacity.
plain_english: When training a massive model, your GPU runs out of memory. Instead of stopping, you can write some data to fast storage on disk and read it back when needed. It's like using your laptop's disk as extra RAM—much slower, but it lets you fit things that wouldn't fit otherwise.
how_it_works: DeepSpeed ZeRO-Infinity pages optimizer states (and optionally parameters and gradients) to NVMe during training. When the GPU needs that data, it reads it back from disk. NVMe bandwidth is ~7 GB/s per drive, PCIe 5.0 ~64 GB/s—orders of magnitude slower than HBM. You configure which tensors offload, how aggressively, and on which devices. The tradeoff is severe: expect 10–100x throughput reduction in exchange for fitting models that otherwise won't fit.
why_it_matters: NVMe offload makes infeasible fine-tunes run on modest hardware. For teams without access to unlimited GPUs, it's the difference between training a 70B parameter model and not training it at all. Speed matters less than the ability to iterate.
example: A researcher fine-tuning Mixtral 8x7B on a single A100 with 80GB HBM. Optimizer state alone exceeds GPU memory. Enabling ZeRO-Infinity offloads the Adam state to a 2TB NVMe drive on the same machine. Training proceeds at 500 tokens/sec instead of 5000, but it completes in days instead of being impossible.
pitfalls: NVMe bandwidth bottleneck makes training 10–100x slower; impractical for frontier research timelines.
PCIe saturation occurs quickly; multiple GPUs or models on the same NVMe compete for bus bandwidth.
Offloading parameters (not just optimizer state) increases disk wear and latency; use sparingly.
No speedup if your bottleneck is compute (e.g., dense attention)—only helps memory pressure.
when_use: Use NVMe offload when you must fit a model on available GPUs but can tolerate multi-day training and have fast local or PCIe-attached NVMe storage.
when_avoid: Avoid when you need sub-day training iteration, have limited NVMe bandwidth, or can provision additional GPUs—the speed penalty rarely justifies the complexity.

faq.q: How much slower is NVMe offload than keeping everything on GPU?
faq.a: Typical slowdown is 10–100x depending on model size, batch size, and NVMe bandwidth. A 500 tokens/sec run becomes 50–5000 tokens/sec. The exact cost depends on how often data is swapped.

faq.q: Is NVMe offload the same as CPU offloading?
faq.a: No. CPU offloading spills to host RAM (slower than GPU HBM but faster than NVMe). NVMe offload goes to disk. NVMe is last resort when RAM is also full.

faq.q: Can I use NVMe offload on multi-GPU setups?
faq.a: Yes, but PCIe becomes the bottleneck quickly. A single NVMe drive shared across 8 GPUs will saturate much faster than a single GPU, further degrading throughput.

faq.q: What should I offload—optimizer state, parameters, or gradients?
faq.a: Start with optimizer state only (smallest performance hit). Offload parameters only if you absolutely must fit the model; offloading gradients is rarely necessary and adds severe latency.

faq.q: Does NVMe offload work with fine-tuning or only pretraining?
faq.a: Works for both. Fine-tuning with large models (Mixtral, Llama 70B) on consumer GPUs is one of the main practical use cases.

title: Docker (ML Containers)
tldr: Container format that packages ML code, models, dependencies, and runtime into reproducible images you deploy anywhere.
plain_english: Docker is like a shrink-wrapped box for your ML project. Instead of telling someone "install Python 3.11, PyTorch 2.0, CUDA 12.1, and these 47 packages," you hand them one file that already contains everything. They run it, and it works the same way on their laptop, a cloud server, or a Kubernetes cluster.
how_it_works: You write a Dockerfile—a recipe listing base image (e.g., nvidia/cuda:12.1), dependencies, model weights, and inference code. Docker builds this into a layered image, caching each step. When you run it, Docker creates a container: an isolated process with its own filesystem, environment variables, and (if you allow it) GPU access via the NVIDIA Container Toolkit. The image is static; containers are running instances. You push images to registries (Docker Hub, ECR, GCR), pull them on any machine, and run them identically.
why_it_matters: ML systems fail in production because "it works on my machine" doesn't hold. Docker eliminates environment drift: same Python version, same CUDA, same model serialization format, everywhere. Kubernetes, most cloud ML services, and CI/CD pipelines all expect Docker images. Skipping this costs weeks of debugging and deployment failures.
example: A team trains a model locally with PyTorch 2.0 and cuDNN 8.8. They write a Dockerfile, build an image, and push it to AWS ECR. Later, they deploy 50 replicas in EKS—Kubernetes pulls that exact image on each node and starts containers. No version mismatches, no GPU driver incompatibility, no missing dependencies.
pitfalls: Large images (>5 GB) slow deployment and waste bandwidth; use multi-stage builds and strip debug symbols from CUDA.
Pinning base image tags like `nvidia/cuda:latest` makes builds non-deterministic; use specific versions like `nvidia/cuda:12.1-runtime-ubuntu22.04`.
Copying entire directories into images bloats them; use `.dockerignore` to exclude notebooks, raw data, and git history.
Running as root in containers is a security risk; create a non-root user if the image is exposed to untrusted input.
when_use: Use Docker when you need reproducibility across machines, plan to deploy on Kubernetes or cloud ML platforms, or work in teams where environment consistency matters.
when_avoid: Avoid Docker for single-machine experimentation where speed trumps reproducibility, or if your stack doesn't support containerization (legacy systems, certain embedded hardware).

faq.q: What's the difference between a Docker image and a container?
faq.a: An image is a blueprint (the Dockerfile you build). A container is a running instance of that image—think of it as the actual process with its own isolated filesystem and environment. You can run many containers from one image.

faq.q: Do I need NVIDIA Container Toolkit to use GPUs in Docker?
faq.a: Yes. The NVIDIA Container Toolkit intercepts GPU requests from inside the container and maps them to the host GPU. Without it, containers can't see GPUs even if the host has them.

faq.q: Why is my Docker image 8 GB when my model is only 2 GB?
faq.a: Base images (e.g., nvidia/cuda) include CUDA libraries, cuDNN, and a full OS. Use multi-stage builds: compile in a builder stage, copy only artifacts to a lean runtime stage. Also exclude large files with `.dockerignore`.

faq.q: Can I run Docker images locally and in production identically?
faq.a: Yes—that's the point. The same image runs on your laptop, a colleague's machine, and production servers. Environment variables and volume mounts can change per deployment, but the core software is identical.

faq.q: What's the difference between Docker and virtual machines?
faq.a: Containers share the host OS kernel and are lightweight (seconds to start, MB in size). VMs include a full OS and are heavier. For ML, containers are faster and more efficient.

title: DSPy
tldr: Framework for building LLM pipelines where prompts and few-shot examples are optimized automatically against a metric, not hand-crafted.
plain_english: DSPy treats your LLM pipeline like a machine-learning model. Instead of writing and tweaking prompts by hand, you define what your modules should do (input/output types), compose them together, and let an optimizer tune the prompts and examples using training data—much like training a neural network. When you swap models or add new data, the pipeline auto-adjusts.
how_it_works: You write modular code where each component has a typed signature (what it takes in, what it outputs). DSPy's optimizer—called a 'teleprompter'—then runs your pipeline on training data, measures outputs against your metric (e.g., accuracy), and iteratively improves the prompts and few-shot examples to boost that metric. If you switch from GPT-4 to Mistral, you re-run the optimizer and get new prompts tailored to that model. Complex tasks like multi-hop question-answering benefit most because many modules can be tuned in concert.
why_it_matters: Hand-crafting prompts doesn't scale: each model change or domain shift forces rewrites. DSPy moves prompt engineering from art into engineering—reproducible, measurable, and automatic. Teams treating LLM apps as products (not demos) avoid the prompt-tuning tax and ship pipelines that adapt as models evolve.
example: A team building a retrieval-augmented QA system defines one module for retrieval and another for answer generation. They write a simple metric (did the answer match the gold answer?), point DSPy at 200 training examples, and run a teleprompter. DSPy finds that Claude 3.5 works best with chain-of-thought in the retrieval prompt but not the generation prompt—and encodes that in the compiled pipeline. A month later, when Claude 4 ships, they re-compile in minutes.
pitfalls: Assuming DSPy works without clean training data and a clear metric; it optimizes prompts toward your metric, so bad training data or vague metrics produce bad results.
Forgetting that teleprompter runs are offline; you must have representative training examples before compilation, not discovering issues in production.
Treating DSPy as a black box; if optimization doesn't improve your metric, you need to debug the module composition or metric definition, not just re-run the teleprompter.
Overcomplicating pipelines; DSPy scales to large chains, but simpler designs with fewer modules often optimize faster and generalize better.
when_use: Use DSPy when you have a multi-step LLM pipeline, a clear success metric, and ≥50–100 training examples to optimize against; it shines for ranking, extraction, multi-hop reasoning, or conditional routing tasks.
when_avoid: Avoid DSPy for one-off prompts, prototypes without data, or tasks where prompt quality is already excellent; the overhead of defining signatures and running optimization isn't worth it for simple use cases.

faq.q: How is DSPy different from prompt engineering?
faq.a: Prompt engineering is manual: you write and refine prompts by trial and error. DSPy is systematic: you define module signatures and metrics, then an optimizer tunes prompts and examples automatically using data. It replaces ad-hoc tuning with reproducible compilation.

faq.q: Does DSPy work without a training dataset?
faq.a: No. DSPy's teleprompter optimizes prompts by measuring success on training examples. Without at least 50–100 labeled examples and a clear metric, there's nothing to optimize toward. For prototyping without data, use standard prompt engineering first.

faq.q: Can I use DSPy with open-source models?
faq.a: Yes. DSPy works with any model exposed via an API or local endpoint—Claude, GPT-4, Llama, Mistral, etc. The optimizer finds the best prompts for whichever model you wire in, then you can swap models and re-compile.

faq.q: How long does DSPy optimization take?
faq.a: Teleprompter runs typically take minutes to hours depending on pipeline complexity, dataset size, and model API latency. A simple two-module pipeline on 200 examples might compile in 5–10 minutes; larger systems take longer. You run this offline, not in production.

faq.q: Is DSPy the same as LangChain?
faq.a: No. LangChain is a framework for building LLM applications with chains, memory, and retrieval. DSPy is a framework for *optimizing* those applications by tuning prompts and examples. You can use both together—DSPy optimizes the pipelines, LangChain orchestrates them.

title: Durable Workflows
tldr: Execution engines that save workflow state so long-running agents restart from their last step, not from scratch.
plain_english: Think of a durable workflow like a save-point in a video game. When your AI agent crashes mid-task—whether from a server restart, network failure, or timeout—it picks up exactly where it left off instead of starting over. Temporal, Inngest, and Dagster are the main tools that handle this. Each records what completed, what failed, and what needs retry, so you don't lose progress on expensive operations.
how_it_works: The engine stores the state and results of each step in a persistent database. When a step finishes, the result is memoized; if the step reruns, it skips the computation and uses the saved result. On crash, the workflow replays completed steps instantly (using recorded outputs) and resumes from the first failed or pending step. Retries, timeouts, and error handling are baked in. Different engines use different mechanics: Temporal uses event sourcing and replay; Inngest uses function-level checkpoints; Dagster treats data assets as the source of truth.
why_it_matters: Long-running agents—research tasks, multi-turn data pipelines, complex reasoning loops—are fragile without durability. A 30-minute agent run that crashes at minute 29 loses all prior work and API calls without this. Durable workflows make agent systems reliable at production scale and reduce wasted compute and API costs from retries.
example: An agentic research task uses Temporal to orchestrate 10 steps: fetch web data, summarize, call Claude API, store results, generate report. If the process crashes at step 7, Temporal replays steps 1–6 from logs (no re-fetching, no re-summarizing) and resumes from step 7. The entire pipeline can be paused, inspected, and resumed—critical for expensive LLM calls and multi-hour workflows.
pitfalls: Assuming memoization is free: replaying steps is fast, but designing idempotent operations (side effects safe to repeat) is mandatory.
Not setting realistic retry budgets: exponential backoff can exhaust quotas on external APIs if a step fails repeatedly.
Choosing the wrong tool for your architecture: Temporal suits complex long-running workflows; Inngest is better for distributed functions; Dagster excels at data-heavy DAGs.
Ignoring observability: without logging and tracing, debugging a replayed workflow becomes opaque.
when_use: Use durable workflows when you have multi-step agent tasks, long-running processes (>5 minutes), costly external API calls, or infrastructure that cannot guarantee uptime.
when_avoid: Avoid for simple stateless requests, real-time streaming, or systems where replay cost exceeds retry cost.

faq.q: What's the difference between Temporal, Inngest, and Dagster?
faq.a: Temporal is event-sourcing based and best for complex, long-running workflows with strict ordering. Inngest is function-first and designed for distributed serverless. Dagster is asset-centric, treating data outputs as the first-class abstraction. Pick based on your task model: orchestration (Temporal), serverless functions (Inngest), or data pipelines (Dagster).

faq.q: Do I need durable workflows for my AI agent?
faq.a: If your agent task takes >5 minutes, calls expensive APIs, or runs on infrastructure that restarts, yes. For short, stateless requests, no. Assess the cost of a crash (lost API calls, recompute) against the overhead of durability.

faq.q: How much slower is a replayed workflow?
faq.a: Replay of completed steps is near-instant because it uses logged results, not recomputation. The bottleneck is I/O to the state store and network latency, typically negligible compared to the original step cost.

faq.q: Can I use durable workflows with LangChain or CrewAI?
faq.a: Yes. Inngest and Temporal integrate with LangChain via wrapper functions; CrewAI works with any executor. Dagster requires more structural changes but can orchestrate LangChain agents as tasks.

faq.q: What happens if my step is not idempotent?
faq.a: If a step has side effects (sending an email, charging a card) and reruns, those effects repeat. You must design steps to be idempotent—deduplication, conditional checks, or idempotency tokens—or accept the risk of duplication.

title: Dynamic Batching
tldr: Collect incoming requests over a short time window, then run inference on the entire batch at once.
plain_english: Instead of waiting for a fixed number of requests before processing, dynamic batching groups whatever arrives within a brief time window (say, 10 milliseconds) and processes them together. It's like a restaurant collecting orders for a few seconds, then cooking them in one batch instead of one at a time. The tradeoff: slightly higher latency per request, but much better hardware utilization.
how_it_works: A request queue holds incoming inference calls. The serving engine waits for either max_queue_delay (e.g., 10 ms) or max_batch_size (e.g., 32 requests) to be reached, whichever comes first. When either threshold hits, all queued requests run through the model in one forward pass, then responses return. Parameters are tunable per workload—tighter windows lower latency but reduce batch efficiency; looser windows improve throughput but increase tail latency.
why_it_matters: Dynamic batching squeezes GPU utilization without blocking requests indefinitely. For short-inference tasks (embeddings, classification, image models), the latency penalty is negligible compared to the throughput gain. This directly cuts serving costs and lets a single replica handle more concurrent users.
example: A recommendation service processes embedding queries. With dynamic batching (10 ms window, batch size 64), incoming requests queue briefly, then 60 queries run together on one GPU pass in ~15 ms total. Without batching, 60 sequential calls might take 600 ms. The 10 ms wait is invisible to users, but throughput increases 8–10x.
pitfalls: Tuning max_queue_delay too high backpressures all requests; too low defeats batching and wastes GPU cycles on small batches.
Works poorly for variable-length inputs (LLM token sequences) because padding wastes compute; continuous batching is better.
Assumes stateless, short-lived inference; unsuitable for long-running or stateful operations like streaming text generation.
Bursty traffic patterns (idle silence then sudden spike) mean some requests wait full window even though batch is small.
when_use: Use dynamic batching for latency-tolerant, short-inference workloads (embeddings, classification, image encoders) where 10–50 ms of queueing is acceptable and batch size is predictable.
when_avoid: Avoid for LLM decoding, streaming outputs, or ultra-low-latency requirements where every millisecond matters; use continuous batching or request-level optimization instead.

faq.q: What's the difference between dynamic batching and continuous batching?
faq.a: Dynamic batching waits for a time window or batch size threshold, then runs all requests at once. Continuous batching interleaves new requests with ongoing decoding, keeping the GPU fed per token. Dynamic is simpler; continuous is more efficient for long sequences.

faq.q: Does dynamic batching add latency to every request?
faq.a: Yes, up to max_queue_delay. A request arriving just after the batch launches must wait ~10 ms. For short tasks, this is acceptable; for real-time systems, tune the window tighter or accept fewer batching benefits.

faq.q: Can I use dynamic batching for LLM text generation?
faq.a: Technically yes, but it's inefficient. LLM decoding is auto-regressive—each token depends on all prior ones. Dynamic batching stalls waiting for the window, wasting GPU time on padding. Continuous batching or speculative decoding is better.

faq.q: How do I tune max_queue_delay and max_batch_size?
faq.a: Start with max_queue_delay around 5–50 ms and max_batch_size matching your GPU memory (typically 32–128 for embeddings). Measure p95 latency and throughput under realistic traffic; increase delay if latency is acceptable and throughput is low; decrease if latency is too high.

faq.q: Does dynamic batching work with variable-length inputs?
faq.a: Yes, but you must pad shorter sequences to the longest in the batch, wasting compute. Fixed-length or similar-length inputs work best. For highly variable sequences, consider bucketing by length or switching to continuous batching.

title: ExLlama (ExLlamaV2)
tldr: Fast GPU inference engine for quantized Llama models, optimized for consumer Nvidia hardware.
plain_english: ExLlamaV2 is software that runs large language models on your gaming GPU (like a 3090 or 4090) much faster than the standard approach. It does this by working exclusively with pre-compressed models and using techniques fine-tuned for Nvidia's consumer hardware. Think of it as a specialized race car engine that only runs on one type of fuel—but when it does, it's exceptionally quick.
how_it_works: ExLlamaV2 accepts quantized Llama models in GPTQ or EXL2 format and loads them onto your GPU's memory (VRAM). The EXL2 format lets you mix different bit-widths (2–8 bits) per layer, trading off speed against quality without recompressing. During inference, it uses fused GPU kernels and optimized memory access patterns to maximize tokens-per-second. It integrates with front-ends like Text Generation WebUI and TabbyAPI, which handle the chat interface and request queuing. The library skips CPU involvement almost entirely, keeping all computation on the GPU.
why_it_matters: On a single consumer GPU, ExLlamaV2 often delivers 2–3× higher throughput than standard inference engines like Ollama or vLLM when running quantized models. For budget-conscious deployments—RP bots, local chat, content generation—this speed difference lets you serve real users without enterprise hardware or cloud costs. Faster inference also means lower latency, which improves perceived responsiveness.
example: A small indie game studio building an in-game NPC dialogue system loads a 7B-parameter Llama model quantized to EXL2 format on a single RTX 4090. ExLlamaV2 delivers responses in <500ms per turn, enabling fluid conversation. Using the same model in standard inference would take 2–3 seconds per response, making gameplay feel unresponsive. The studio saves $200+/month in inference API costs and avoids network latency.
pitfalls: EXL2 quantization is more complex than GPTQ and requires careful bit-width allocation; poor choices can degrade quality noticeably.
ExLlamaV2 only supports Nvidia GPUs (no AMD, no Apple Silicon); incompatible hardware makes the library unusable.
Memory footprint varies sharply by bit-width; a model quantized at 5 bits fits comfortably on 24GB VRAM, but 8-bit pushes past it.
Mixed-precision EXL2 models are less portable; not all quantization tools support every bit-width combination consistently.
when_use: Use ExLlamaV2 when you need maximum tokens-per-second on a single consumer Nvidia GPU and your models are already quantized to GPTQ or EXL2 format.
when_avoid: Avoid if you run on non-Nvidia hardware, need to run full-precision models, or prioritize portability over raw throughput.

faq.q: Is ExLlamaV2 the same as GGUF or llama.cpp?
faq.a: No. ExLlamaV2 is Nvidia-GPU–only and supports GPTQ/EXL2 formats; llama.cpp (GGUF) is cross-platform and runs on CPU. ExLlamaV2 is typically faster on supported hardware.

faq.q: What GPU do I need for ExLlamaV2?
faq.a: Any Nvidia GPU with at least 8GB VRAM (RTX 3060 or newer recommended). A 3090, 4090, or H100 gives the best throughput; RTX 4080 or L40S work well for smaller models.

faq.q: Can I run ExLlamaV2 on AMD or Apple Silicon?
faq.a: No. ExLlamaV2 requires Nvidia CUDA architecture. AMD users should consider vLLM or llama.cpp; Apple users should use MLX.

faq.q: What's the difference between EXL2 and GPTQ in ExLlamaV2?
faq.a: EXL2 supports mixed bit-widths per layer (finer control); GPTQ uses uniform quantization. EXL2 offers better quality-to-speed tradeoffs but is less standardized.

faq.q: How much faster is ExLlamaV2 compared to Ollama?
faq.a: On equivalent quantized models, ExLlamaV2 typically delivers 50–200% more tokens-per-second, depending on model size and bit-width.

title: FastAPI (LLM Backends)
tldr: Python web framework designed for async LLM APIs with automatic input validation and OpenAPI documentation.
plain_english: FastAPI is a web server framework that handles incoming requests and sends responses back over HTTP. Think of it as a traffic controller for your LLM—it receives user queries, validates them, routes them to the actual model (running elsewhere), and streams responses back. It was built from the ground up for async work, meaning it can handle hundreds of concurrent requests without blocking.
how_it_works: You write route handlers in FastAPI that accept request bodies (validated by Pydantic schemas) and return responses. When a request arrives, FastAPI deserializes JSON, checks it against your schema, and rejects invalid data before it reaches your code. For LLM use cases, you typically call an external model service (vLLM, OpenAI API, etc.) inside these handlers, then stream or return the result. FastAPI runs on an ASGI server like Uvicorn, which handles concurrency natively. The framework auto-generates OpenAPI docs from your code, so clients see live API documentation without extra work.
why_it_matters: LLM APIs need to handle high concurrency (multiple users streaming tokens simultaneously) while validating inputs consistently. FastAPI's async design and built-in validation eliminate boilerplate and prevent common bugs. It's become the default choice for production LLM backends, so knowing it is expected in production Python roles.
example: A team building a RAG chatbot uses FastAPI to expose their retrieval pipeline. Clients POST questions to `/chat` with a Pydantic schema requiring `{"query": str, "max_tokens": int}`. FastAPI validates the request, passes it to their vLLM instance, and yields tokens back via Server-Sent Events. The same route handler also logs requests to a monitoring service and enforces rate limits—all with simple decorators.
pitfalls: Blocking calls inside route handlers (e.g., synchronous database queries) will starve concurrent requests; use async libraries or run_in_threadpool().
Forgetting to set appropriate timeouts on downstream LLM calls, causing clients to hang indefinitely if the model service stalls.
Not streaming responses for long-running LLM calls, forcing the client to wait for the full completion before receiving anything.
Deploying a single-worker Uvicorn instance; always run multiple workers or use an async worker model to handle real load.
when_use: Use FastAPI when building HTTP APIs for LLM services, especially if you need concurrent request handling, auto-validated request/response schemas, or built-in async support.
when_avoid: Avoid FastAPI if your LLM service is synchronous only and latency-insensitive, or if you're running in a non-Python environment where native frameworks are more natural.

faq.q: How do I stream LLM responses in FastAPI?
faq.a: Use `StreamingResponse` with a generator function that yields chunks. For Server-Sent Events, yield newline-delimited JSON with the `text/event-stream` media type. FastAPI will flush each chunk to the client immediately rather than buffering.

faq.q: Can FastAPI handle real-time token streaming?
faq.a: Yes, but you must use async I/O throughout the chain. If your LLM client library (like OpenAI SDK) is sync-only, wrap it in `asyncio.to_thread()` or use an async wrapper to avoid blocking the event loop.

faq.q: What's the difference between FastAPI and Flask for LLM APIs?
faq.a: Flask is synchronous by default and requires explicit async syntax; FastAPI is async-first, ships with Pydantic validation built-in, and auto-generates API docs. For high-concurrency LLM workloads, FastAPI is much more efficient.

faq.q: How do I add rate limiting to a FastAPI LLM endpoint?
faq.a: Use a third-party library like `slowapi` or implement a custom dependency with per-user token buckets. Decorators or middleware can enforce limits before requests reach your model service.

faq.q: Does FastAPI work with async LLM clients?
faq.a: Yes—libraries like `httpx`, `aiohttp`, and newer async-native SDKs integrate seamlessly. This is where FastAPI shines: you can make concurrent calls to multiple model services without blocking.

title: Fireworks AI
tldr: Inference API optimized for fast open-source LLM serving with fine-tuning and structured output support.
plain_english: Fireworks AI is a hosted service that runs open-source language models (like Llama, Mixtral, DeepSeek) on high-speed GPUs, then exposes them via API. Think of it as renting a pre-warmed, tuned inference engine instead of managing your own hardware. You pay per token, get responses in milliseconds, and can customize models without running your own GPUs.
how_it_works: You send a prompt to Fireworks' API endpoint for a model you choose (e.g., Mixtral 8x7B). Their infrastructure routes your request to a GPU cluster optimized for low latency, streaming tokens back to you in real time. You can enable grammar-constrained generation (force JSON output), apply LoRA fine-tuning on the fly, or use function calling for tool integration. Billing is per token consumed—prefill and generation tokens sometimes priced separately.
why_it_matters: Latency matters in production: a 50ms response time beats a 200ms one for chatbots, agents, and real-time classification. Fireworks typically ranks top-tier on throughput benchmarks compared to Together AI and Lambda Labs. It eliminates the capex and ops burden of managing GPU infrastructure while staying cost-competitive with home-grown setups for moderate-to-high volume.
example: A fintech team building a real-time document summarization pipeline for loan applications uses Fireworks to run Mixtral 8x7B with grammar constraints forcing JSON output (loan summary, risk score, action items). At 500 requests/day, they pay ~$2–3/day instead of provisioning an A100 GPU ($2k/month). Average latency is 180ms end-to-end—acceptable for their SLA.
pitfalls: LoRA fine-tuning on Fireworks is model-specific; you cannot load a LoRA trained elsewhere without Fireworks-native tooling.
Outbound API calls (e.g., function calling hitting external APIs) count toward token limits and can inflate costs if not rate-limited.
Context window limits vary per model; Fireworks does not auto-extend context for all open models—check their model card first.
Pricing per token can exceed on-demand GPU rental if your query volume is extremely high and request sizes are large; do break-even math.
when_use: Use Fireworks when you need sub-200ms latency on open-source models, want to avoid GPU ops, or need per-request fine-tuning without model reloading.
when_avoid: Avoid when latency is unconstrained, you run millions of tokens/month (total-cost-of-ownership of a V100+ cluster may be lower), or you require closed-source model APIs.

faq.q: Is Fireworks AI faster than Together AI?
faq.a: Often yes on throughput per benchmarks, but latency depends on model size and your payload. Run a side-by-side test on your exact use case; both are top-tier.

faq.q: Can I use my own fine-tuned weights on Fireworks?
faq.a: Not directly. You can apply LoRA adapters trained with Fireworks' tooling, but custom model binaries must be converted to their format.

faq.q: What happens if I hit the rate limit?
faq.a: Fireworks returns HTTP 429. You must back off and retry; there is no queue. Plan token budgets and concurrency to stay under limits.

faq.q: Does Fireworks support vision models?
faq.a: Yes, they host Qwen VL and LLaVA variants. Vision tokens are priced differently—check their pricing page for image input costs.

faq.q: How do I reduce costs if my bill is too high?
faq.a: Batch requests, use smaller models (Mistral 7B vs. Mixtral 8x7B), enable KV-cache reuse with prefix caching, or switch to a GPU rental if volume justifies it.

title: Flash Attention
tldr: GPU-friendly attention algorithm that runs 2–4× faster with less memory by using on-chip SRAM instead of slow HBM.
plain_english: Standard attention computes a massive N×N matrix that lives in slow GPU memory, bottlenecking training and inference. Flash Attention reorganizes the math to fit chunks in fast on-chip memory (SRAM), run the softmax and multiply together in one operation, then stream results back. You get the exact same answer—it's not an approximation, just smarter memory access.
how_it_works: Flash Attention tiles the query, key, and value matrices into blocks small enough to fit in SRAM. For each block pair, it fuses the softmax normalization and matrix multiply into a single GPU kernel, avoiding the intermediate N×N matrix write to slow HBM. It tracks partial softmax statistics (row-wise max and sum) across tiles and corrects them at the end so the final output is numerically identical to standard attention. FlashAttention-2 and -3 further optimize throughput by using Hopper GPU features like warp-group-level operations.
why_it_matters: Flash Attention reduced training costs for most modern LLMs by 2–3×, making long-context models (8K, 32K, 1M tokens) feasible without O(N²) memory blowing out your VRAM budget. It became the default in PyTorch, Hugging Face Transformers, and vLLM, so you get the benefit automatically—no code changes needed in many frameworks.
example: When training Llama 3.3 70B on 8 H100 GPUs, switching from standard attention to Flash Attention-2 reduced per-token-per-GPU memory usage from ~240 GB/s effective bandwidth utilization to near-peak performance, cutting training time per epoch by 35% without changing the model or data pipeline.
pitfalls: Flash Attention on older GPUs (V100, T4, A100) may not activate; you must explicitly use memory-efficient attention from xformers library or it falls back to the slow path.
Numerical stability differences from standard attention can compound in very long context (>100K tokens), especially with low precision (fp16); use bfloat16 when possible.
Custom attention patterns (e.g., local/banded attention) may not have optimized Flash kernels; check if your framework supports your specific pattern.
Debugging attention weights becomes harder because intermediate matrices are never materialized; use logprob tracking instead of direct matrix inspection.
when_use: Use Flash Attention when training or serving any transformer model on modern GPUs (H100, A100, L40S) where standard attention is your bottleneck and your framework supports it (most do by default now).
when_avoid: Avoid if you're on older hardware (V100, T4), need to inspect intermediate attention matrices for debugging, or use uncommon attention patterns with no Flash kernel implementation.

faq.q: Is Flash Attention mathematically identical to standard attention?
faq.a: Yes. Flash Attention computes the exact same output; it's only different in memory access patterns and kernel fusion. No approximation or accuracy loss.

faq.q: What GPU do I need for Flash Attention?
faq.a: Flash Attention-1 works on most GPUs (A100, H100, L40S). Flash Attention-2/3 are optimized for Ampere (A100) and Hopper (H100) but fall back safely on older hardware.

faq.q: Do I have to change my code to use Flash Attention?
faq.a: Usually no. Hugging Face Transformers and PyTorch enable it by default on compatible hardware. You can set `attn_implementation='flash_attention_2'` explicitly if your framework supports it.

faq.q: How much does Flash Attention speed up training?
faq.a: Typically 2–4× faster on modern GPUs, with the largest gains on longer contexts (8K+ tokens). Actual speedup depends on sequence length, batch size, and hardware.

faq.q: Can I use Flash Attention for inference?
faq.a: Yes, and it's critical for long-context inference. vLLM uses paged-attention (a variant) to keep KV cache in SRAM and reduce memory fragmentation during batched serving.

title: Flash Attention 2 / 3
tldr: GPU-optimized attention algorithms that reduce compute overhead and double training throughput with no code changes.
plain_english: Flash Attention 2 and 3 are rewrites of the attention mechanism—the part of transformers that decides which tokens matter most to each other—to match how modern GPUs actually work. Instead of moving data back and forth between GPU memory levels, these versions keep computation tiled in fast cache and minimize data movement. FA3 further exploits H100-specific hardware features like async copies and FP8 precision.
how_it_works: Standard attention loads query, key, and value matrices from GPU HBM (main memory) many times during computation. Flash Attention 2 tiles the computation so blocks of rows and columns stay in faster SRAM across multiple operations, cutting data movement by ~10x. FA3 (2024) adds tensor memory accelerator (TMA) support for async prefetch and FP8 support, eliminating more stalls. Both are drop-in replacements in PyTorch and other frameworks—you call the same attention function but get 1.5–2x speedup for FA2, another 1.5–2x for FA3 on H100s.
why_it_matters: Attention is the bottleneck in LLM training and inference—it doesn't fully saturate GPU compute. Upgrading from standard attention to FA2/FA3 often delivers 2–4x wall-clock speedup without rewriting model code, retraining, or changing batch size. This directly cuts training time and inference cost at scale.
example: A team training a 7B parameter model on 8x A100 GPUs sees ~120 tokens/second/GPU with standard attention. Switching to Flash Attention 2 jumps that to ~240 tokens/second without touching the training loop. On 8x H100s with FA3, they reach ~400 tokens/second on the same model, cutting a 2-week training run to 5 days.
pitfalls: FA3 requires H100-era hardware and CUDA 12.1+; backporting to A100 or consumer GPUs fails silently or uses slow fallback.
Both versions can produce slightly different numerical results than baseline attention due to reordering of operations; verify task-critical outputs.
Batch size and sequence length can trigger suboptimal tiling paths; profile before committing to long training runs.
Not all attention variants (e.g., relative position biases, custom causal masks) are supported; check framework implementation first.
when_use: Use FA2/FA3 when training or serving transformer models on NVIDIA GPUs and standard attention is your computational bottleneck (common for sequence lengths >512).
when_avoid: Avoid if using non-NVIDIA hardware, custom attention kernels you cannot replace, or when numerical determinism is a hard requirement.

faq.q: What is the difference between Flash Attention 2 and Flash Attention 3?
faq.a: FA2 reduces data movement through tiling; FA3 adds H100-specific features (TMA, async copies, FP8) for another 1.5–2x speedup. FA3 requires H100 hardware; FA2 works on A100 and newer.

faq.q: Do I need to change my code to use Flash Attention 2?
faq.a: No. It's a drop-in replacement in PyTorch and Hugging Face Transformers. Update your framework version and it activates automatically.

faq.q: Will Flash Attention 2 change my model's output?
faq.a: Slightly. The algorithm reorders floating-point operations, so results differ in the last few decimal places. For most tasks this is negligible, but verify on your validation set.

faq.q: Does Flash Attention work on RTX consumer GPUs?
faq.a: FA2 yes, with some overhead. FA3 requires H100. Check your GPU's compute capability and test before relying on it for production.

faq.q: How much faster is Flash Attention 3 than 2?
faq.a: On H100 hardware, FA3 is typically 1.5–2x faster than FA2 for the attention operation itself, though overall model speedup depends on attention's share of total compute.

title: GGUF
tldr: File format for quantized LLMs that run on CPU and GPU, used by Ollama and LM Studio.
plain_english: GGUF is a container format for storing AI model weights and tokenizers in a single file. Think of it like a ZIP file optimized for machine learning—it holds everything you need to run a language model locally, usually in a compressed form to fit on regular computers. You download GGUF files from HuggingFace when using tools like Ollama or LM Studio.
how_it_works: GGUF stores model weights, tokenizer data, and metadata in one file with a specific binary structure. The weights are almost always quantized—compressed using schemes like Q4_K_M or Q5_K_M—which reduces file size from tens of GB to a few GB. Your inference engine (llama.cpp, Ollama, LM Studio) reads the GGUF header to understand the model architecture, loads weights into RAM or VRAM, and uses the embedded tokenizer to convert text to tokens for inference. The quantization lets you run models on CPU when GPU memory isn't available.
why_it_matters: GGUF replaced earlier formats because it's efficient to load and widely supported across open-source inference tools. Running models locally gives you privacy, offline capability, and no per-token API costs—tradeoffs are slower generation speed and hardware constraints.
example: You want to run Llama 2 7B locally on a laptop with 16GB RAM. You download the `Llama-2-7b-Q4_K_M.gguf` file from HuggingFace (about 4GB), load it into Ollama or LM Studio, and start generating text. Q4_K_M quantization loses minimal quality versus the original float32 weights but uses 1/8th the disk and memory.
pitfalls: Different quantization levels (Q2, Q4, Q8) require different VRAM; Q4_K_M is the safe default, but Q2 may hallucinate more on reasoning tasks.
GGUF files are tied to a specific llama.cpp version; very old files may not load in newer inference engines without conversion.
Quantization is one-way; you cannot reconstruct the original float32 weights from a GGUF file, so always keep source models if you need retraining.
CPU inference speed drops sharply below ~7B parameters on older hardware; GPU offloading (even partial) is recommended for real-time use.
when_use: Use GGUF when you need to run a model locally for privacy, cost savings, or offline access, and your hardware has <32GB RAM or limited GPU VRAM.
when_avoid: Avoid GGUF if you need the highest inference speed (API services are faster), if you plan to fine-tune the model (use the original format), or if your model isn't available in GGUF format yet.

faq.q: What does GGUF stand for?
faq.a: GGUF is the GPT-Generated Unified Format. It replaced GGML (GGJT variant) as the standard for llama.cpp-compatible models in late 2023.

faq.q: Can I run GGUF files on GPU?
faq.a: Yes. Most tools like Ollama and LM Studio automatically offload layers to GPU if available, using CUDA or Metal. You can also manually configure the split between CPU and VRAM.

faq.q: What's the difference between Q4_K_M and Q5_K_M?
faq.a: Q5_K_M uses 5-bit quantization and preserves more detail than Q4_K_M (4-bit), but takes up ~20% more disk/memory. For most use cases, Q4_K_M is the sweet spot; use Q5 if quality degrades noticeably.

faq.q: How do I convert a Hugging Face model to GGUF?
faq.a: Use the llama.cpp `convert.py` script: clone the llama.cpp repo, run `python convert.py /path/to/model --outfile model.gguf`, then optionally quantize with `./quantize model.gguf model-q4.gguf Q4_K_M`.

faq.q: Is GGUF faster than ONNX or TensorRT?
faq.a: No; TensorRT (NVIDIA) and ONNX Runtime are often faster for GPU inference. GGUF trades speed for simplicity and CPU compatibility—it's optimized for local, multi-device deployment, not peak throughput.

title: GH200 (Nvidia Grace Hopper)
tldr: Nvidia chip pairing a Grace CPU with H100 GPU via 900 GB/s NVLink for long-context inference and large model serving.
plain_english: The GH200 is a single chip that combines a CPU and GPU on the same piece of silicon, connected by an extremely fast internal highway (NVLink). Instead of data bouncing between separate chips through slow connectors, the CPU and GPU share the same memory pool at full speed. Think of it like upgrading from mail delivery to having workers in the same building.
how_it_works: The GH200 integrates a 72-core ARM Grace CPU, an H100 GPU, and 480 GB of unified HBM memory. The CPU and GPU access the same address space across a 900 GB/s NVLink interconnect, eliminating the PCIe bottleneck. During inference, the CPU can offload KV cache or expert weights to its local memory without stalling, then GPU retrieves them at full bandwidth. This design enables serving models with longer contexts or larger weight sets than HBM alone would fit.
why_it_matters: CPU–GPU offloading patterns that are impractical on PCIe-connected systems become viable at scale. You can serve longer sequences or larger mixture-of-experts models without memory pressure forcing model sharding across multiple GPUs, reducing latency and operational complexity.
example: A team running 8-expert MoE inference (e.g., 1.2T model) on two H100 GPUs would face GPU memory limits. With GH200, the CPU holds inactive expert weights (240 GB cohesive memory) while the GPU computes, then swaps experts at full NVLink speed. Latency per token stays under 100ms even with 32K context, versus >500ms with CPU–GPU PCIe swapping.
pitfalls: NVLink saturation is not automatic; kernel design must explicitly route data through the interconnect—poorly written kernels ignore the bandwidth advantage.
480 GB unified memory includes both CPU and GPU pools; allocating greedily to one side starves the other.
GH200 is NVIDIA-only infrastructure; workloads require Grace Hopper-specific tuning and are not portable to standard GPU clusters.
Cost per inference token may exceed multi-H100 setups if you do not fully utilize the CPU offload advantage.
when_use: Use GH200 when serving very long-context models (>32K tokens), large MoE experts, or embedding-heavy workloads where CPU–GPU memory coherency unlocks significant latency gains.
when_avoid: Avoid GH200 if your workload fits entirely in HBM, your model is standard dense (not MoE), or you need portability across multiple GPU vendors.

faq.q: What is the difference between GH200 and two separate H100 GPUs?
faq.a: GH200 pairs a CPU and GPU on one die with 900 GB/s NVLink; separate H100s connect via PCIe (16 GB/s). GH200 eliminates the bandwidth bottleneck for weight swapping and offload, reducing latency by 5–10× for MoE or long-context workloads.

faq.q: How much memory does GH200 have?
faq.a: 480 GB of HBM + 144 GB of CPU-side memory = 624 GB total unified addressable space. The split between CPU and GPU is configurable depending on your offload pattern.

faq.q: Can I run standard LLM inference code on GH200?
faq.a: Yes, but you won't realize the full benefit. You need kernels and scheduling logic that explicitly leverage CPU–GPU coherency. Stock CUDA kernels treat GH200 like a regular GPU and miss the offload advantage.

faq.q: Is GH200 cheaper than buying multiple GPUs for the same throughput?
faq.a: GH200 has high upfront cost but eliminates multi-GPU interconnect overhead and enables denser model packing. Cost-per-token depends on your workload; CPU offload patterns favor GH200, while dense batch inference may be cheaper on distributed H100s.

faq.q: What companies are using GH200 in production?
faq.a: As of early 2025, GH200 is in early deployment with major cloud providers (Lambda Labs, CoreWeave) and research labs. Adoption is still limited due to availability and specialized tuning requirements.

title: GPU (Graphics Processing Unit)
tldr: Thousands of simple cores optimized for matrix math; required hardware for all modern ML training and inference.
plain_english: A GPU is a processor with thousands of small cores that all do the same math operation on different data at once—like having 10,000 workers doing identical tasks on different rows of a spreadsheet. Neural networks are built from matrix multiplications, which is exactly what GPUs excel at. That's why every LLM and vision model runs on GPUs, not regular CPUs.
how_it_works: GPUs use SIMT (Single Instruction Multiple Thread) architecture: one instruction executes across thousands of cores in parallel. A matrix multiplication in a neural network maps perfectly to this pattern—you compute many dot products simultaneously. CUDA (Nvidia's toolkit) or ROCm (AMD's) compiles your training code to run on specific GPU hardware. Data flows into GPU memory (HBM), computations happen in the cores, and results return to main memory. Specialized accelerators like TPUs and Trainium optimize specific workloads (matrix operations for Google, distributed training for AWS) but follow the same principle.
why_it_matters: Your GPU choice directly constrains model size, training speed, and inference cost. An H100 can train LLaMA 70B; an A100 cannot. Inference on a single A6000 costs 10× more per token than a distributed H100 cluster. For most teams, GPU procurement and selection is the single largest cost lever and technical bottleneck in production.
example: A team fine-tuning Llama 2 7B on their own data needs at least one A100 (80GB) or two L40S cards. Training takes ~3 hours on one A100 but 12+ hours on consumer GPUs. At inference, they run vLLM on the same A100 to batch 50 concurrent requests. Switching to H100s would halve training time but triple monthly cloud costs—the ROI depends on throughput demands.
pitfalls: Assuming all GPUs are interchangeable: H100 and A100 have different memory architectures and tensor core designs; workload profiling is mandatory.
Ignoring memory bandwidth: A 100GB model with poor data locality on an L40S will be slower than an A100 with worse compute specs.
Forgetting HBM costs more than VRAM: GPU memory is 5–10× the price of CPU RAM; underprovisioning forces gradient checkpointing overhead.
Treating single-GPU training as a proxy for distributed training: NCCL communication, data parallelism, and pipeline parallelism introduce nonlinear scaling penalties.
when_use: Use GPUs whenever you train or serve neural networks, run embeddings at scale, or need sub-100ms inference latency.
when_avoid: Avoid GPUs for sparse, CPU-friendly workloads (tree search, regex, classical ML on tabular data) or when inference SLA permits batch-only processing with longer latencies.

faq.q: What's the difference between H100 and A100 GPUs?
faq.a: H100 has newer tensor cores and higher memory bandwidth (3.3 TB/s vs 2.0 TB/s), making it 1.5–2× faster for transformer training. A100 is cheaper and sufficient for inference or smaller models. H100 dominates distributed LLM training; A100 dominates cost-conscious fine-tuning.

faq.q: Do I need a GPU or can I use a TPU instead?
faq.a: TPUs excel at dense matrix multiplication (Google's domain) but have limited availability outside GCP. GPUs (Nvidia, AMD) are widely available, have mature software ecosystems (CUDA, PyTorch), and work for nearly every ML task. TPUs are a niche optimization for Google-scale training.

faq.q: How much GPU memory do I need for LLM fine-tuning?
faq.a: A 7B model with full fine-tuning needs ~24–32GB GPU memory. LoRA reduces this to 8–16GB. Quantization (QLoRA) fits on 8GB but adds latency. Rule of thumb: allocate 4× the model's parameter count in GB for training, 2× for inference.

faq.q: Why is CUDA required for GPU training?
faq.a: CUDA compiles your PyTorch or TensorFlow code into GPU machine code. It's also Nvidia's proprietary ecosystem—AMD uses ROCm, and other vendors have custom compilers. Without it, your code runs on CPU, which is 10–100× slower.

faq.q: Can I train an LLM on consumer GPUs like RTX 4090?
faq.a: Yes, but only smaller models or with heavy optimization. An RTX 4090 (24GB) trains 7B models with LoRA in days. Full pretraining of 70B+ requires enterprise GPUs. Consumer cards also lack fault tolerance and NVLink, making distributed training painful.

title: Gradio
tldr: Python library that turns any ML function into a shareable web interface in minutes.
plain_english: Gradio wraps a Python function—like a classifier or image generator—in a web UI without you writing HTML or CSS. You define inputs (text, image, audio), outputs, and call .launch() to instantly get a link you can share. Think of it as a bridge between your model code and people who want to test it.
how_it_works: You pass a Python function, input components, and output components to gr.Interface(). Gradio generates a form that collects input, calls your function, and displays the result in a browser. It handles component rendering, event wiring, and creates a public tunnel link (via Gradio's servers) so anyone can access your demo without hosting. You can also build custom interfaces using gr.Blocks for more complex layouts and state management.
why_it_matters: Gradio eliminates deployment friction for model demos, making it the fastest path from training to shareable prototype. It's the default demo layer for Hugging Face Spaces, enabling researchers and teams to collect feedback, run live tests, and iterate without backend infrastructure overhead.
example: A computer vision researcher trains a custom object detector and wants feedback from collaborators. Instead of building a Flask app, she wraps the model in Gradio (5 lines of code), clicks Share, and emails the link. Non-technical stakeholders click Upload Image, see bounding boxes in seconds, and comment on accuracy—all without downloading code or installing dependencies.
pitfalls: Relying on Gradio's public tunnel for production: links are temporary and not suitable for critical workflows or sensitive data.
Overloading .launch() with compute-heavy functions blocks the event loop; use background tasks or async patterns for long-running inference.
Not versioning the Gradio app with your model: component changes or API updates can break shared links if you iterate without tracking versions.
Assuming Gradio scales for high concurrency: it's a demo tool, not a production inference server; use Gradio + TensorFlow Serving or vLLM for real load.
when_use: Use Gradio when you want a quick, shareable interface for a single model or function, need to gather user feedback on prototypes, or are demoing work on Hugging Face Spaces.
when_avoid: Avoid Gradio if you need fine-grained control over UI/UX, require production-grade multi-model serving, handle sensitive data, or need real-time collaborative features.

faq.q: Can I use Gradio with a trained model?
faq.a: Yes. Load your model (PyTorch, TensorFlow, JAX) inside the function you pass to Gradio, then define inputs/outputs matching your model's signature.

faq.q: Is Gradio free to use?
faq.a: Gradio itself is free and open-source. Public share links are free but temporary (7 days); persistent hosting requires deploying to Hugging Face Spaces, a cloud provider, or your own server.

faq.q: How does Gradio compare to Streamlit?
faq.a: Streamlit is general-purpose (dashboards, data apps); Gradio is optimized for model demos. Gradio launches faster and has built-in input/output components for ML. Streamlit offers more layout control.

faq.q: Can I deploy a Gradio app on my own server?
faq.a: Yes. Use gr.launch(share=False) locally, or containerize with Docker and deploy to Kubernetes, AWS, GCP, or any host that supports Python WSGI servers.

faq.q: Does Gradio handle file uploads and downloads?
faq.a: Yes. Gradio natively supports File, Image, Audio, Video, and other input types. You can return files or processed outputs directly from your function.

title: Groq (LPU)
tldr: Custom chips optimized for LLM inference, delivering 500+ tokens per second with predictable latency.
plain_english: Groq builds specialized processors (Language Processing Units) designed from scratch to run LLM inference fast. Instead of using general-purpose GPUs, Groq's chips have fixed memory layouts and deterministic execution to eliminate unpredictable slowdowns. You access them through Groq Cloud, which hosts models like Llama and Mixtral.
how_it_works: Groq LPUs use a deterministic architecture: tokens flow through the chip in a predictable pattern, avoiding the dynamic memory access bottlenecks that slow down GPUs. The chip pre-allocates space for activations and weights, eliminating cache misses. During inference, you send a prompt to Groq Cloud, the LPU processes it token-by-token with fixed timing per token, and you receive output at 500+ tokens/second. Trade-off: Groq currently supports fewer models and doesn't yet handle the largest new models.
why_it_matters: For latency-sensitive applications—voice assistants, real-time chat, or interactive agents—Groq's speed can cut response time by 5–10x versus GPU-based serving. This matters when user experience depends on sub-100ms latency or when cost per inference is driven by compute time.
example: A voice AI startup using Groq Cloud can respond to voice input in under 500ms end-to-end (including speech recognition and generation), whereas the same pipeline on A100 GPUs would take 2–3 seconds. This speed allows conversational feel without expensive multi-GPU clusters.
pitfalls: Groq's model roster is smaller than vLLM or together.ai; you may not find your fine-tuned model available.
Deterministic design trades off model flexibility—you cannot easily experiment with custom kernels or modify inference behavior mid-flight.
Lock-in risk: migrating to another provider requires rewriting integration code since Groq's API differs from standard OpenAI-compatible endpoints.
Cost-per-token may be higher than spot GPU instances if your latency tolerance is >500ms.
when_use: Use Groq when you need sub-1-second LLM responses at scale, your model is in their supported list (Llama, Mixtral, etc.), and you can afford vendor lock-in.
when_avoid: Avoid Groq if you need the latest frontier models (GPT-4, Claude Opus), require custom fine-tuned weights, or have flexible latency budgets where GPU batch inference is cheaper.

faq.q: How fast is Groq compared to GPUs?
faq.a: Groq delivers 500+ tokens/second on Llama or Mixtral, typically 5–10x faster than A100 or H100 GPU inference serving for the same model. Speed comes from deterministic chip design, not just clock frequency.

faq.q: What models can I run on Groq?
faq.a: Groq Cloud currently hosts Llama (2, 3, 3.1), Mixtral, and a handful of other open models. Newer frontier models (GPT-4, Claude 3.5) are not available. You cannot upload custom fine-tuned weights.

faq.q: Is Groq cheaper than other inference APIs?
faq.a: Groq's per-token cost is competitive but not always the lowest; however, total cost per interaction is often lower because you finish faster and need fewer retries or fallbacks.

faq.q: Do I need to change my code to use Groq?
faq.a: Yes—Groq's API does not match OpenAI's chat completions endpoint exactly. You will need to rewrite client code or use a gateway like LiteLLM to normalize requests.

faq.q: When should I use Groq instead of vLLM or TensorRT?
faq.a: Use Groq when you need the lowest latency (sub-500ms) and accept model limitations and vendor lock-in. Use vLLM or TensorRT if you need flexibility, custom models, or multi-model serving on your own infra.

title: GQA (Grouped Query Attention)
tldr: Attention variant where multiple query heads share key/value heads, cutting KV cache memory without quality loss.
plain_english: Standard attention gives each head its own query, key, and value projections. GQA groups multiple query heads to share one set of key/value projections—like having eight people read from one shared notebook instead of each carrying their own. This cuts memory use during inference while keeping output quality nearly identical.
how_it_works: Multi-head attention computes N independent attention heads, each with separate Q, K, V projections. GQA partitions the N query heads into G groups; all heads in a group share one K, V projection but keep separate Q projections. A common setup: 8 query heads → 1 KV head (8× KV cache reduction). During inference, you compute attention once per group instead of once per head, then replicate results across grouped query heads. Llama 2 70B, Llama 3, and most modern LLMs use this by default.
why_it_matters: KV cache is the primary memory bottleneck in inference. GQA cuts that cost linearly without harming model quality, making long-context generation and large batches tractable on fixed hardware. It's now standard because the tradeoff is one-way: free wins.
example: A team deploying Llama 3 70B on an A100 for a chat API serving 100 concurrent users. With Multi-Query Attention (MQA, extreme GQA), KV cache per request would be ~200 MB; with full 64-head attention, it'd be ~1.6 GB. GQA at 8 query heads to 1 KV head reduces it to ~400 MB—enough to fit more requests in memory and reduce latency.
pitfalls: GQA with too few KV heads (e.g., 32 query to 1 KV) can degrade quality; find your model's sweet spot experimentally.
Sharing KV heads means grouped heads attend to identical contexts—useful for efficiency, but limits head diversity within a group.
Not all architectures support GQA; older models or custom implementations may not benefit without retraining.
Inference frameworks must support grouped-query logic; some older quantization or serving tools don't handle it correctly.
when_use: Use GQA when you need to reduce inference memory or serve long contexts on limited hardware, and your model is already built with it (Llama 2/3, Mistral, Qwen).
when_avoid: Avoid if your model was not trained with GQA—retrofitting requires retraining. Also skip if your inference framework doesn't support it natively.

faq.q: What is the difference between GQA and Multi-Query Attention?
faq.a: MQA is GQA's extreme case: all N query heads share one KV head. GQA groups query heads (e.g., 8 to 1), balancing memory savings with quality. MQA saves more memory but may hurt output; GQA is the practical middle ground.

faq.q: Does GQA reduce model quality?
faq.a: Minimal impact if tuned correctly. Research shows well-tuned GQA (e.g., 8 query heads per KV head) preserves quality while cutting KV cache 8×. Too few KV heads can degrade accuracy; most models are trained to a sweet spot.

faq.q: Can I add GQA to a model that wasn't trained with it?
faq.a: Not directly without retraining. GQA must be baked into the architecture from pretraining. You can't retrofit it to an existing full-attention model without losing performance.

faq.q: How much does GQA actually speed up inference?
faq.a: Speedup depends on batch size and context length. On large batches or long sequences, KV cache bandwidth is the bottleneck—GQA directly cuts it by the grouping ratio. Single-token generation may see little speedup; batched or long-context workloads see 20–40% latency gains.

faq.q: Do all modern LLMs use GQA?
faq.a: Most modern models (Llama 2/3, Mistral, Qwen, Command-R) use some form of GQA or MQA. Older models (GPT-3, early Llama) and some research models use full multi-head attention. Check model docs to confirm.

title: H100 (Nvidia Hopper)
tldr: Nvidia's Hopper GPU with 80GB memory and 3 TB/s bandwidth; default training hardware for frontier LLMs through 2024.
plain_english: The H100 is a data-center GPU—a specialized chip for running AI workloads at scale. Think of it like a factory assembly line optimized for one specific task: matrix multiplication at speed. If you're renting cloud compute to train a large language model, you're almost certainly getting H100s.
how_it_works: The H100 packs 141 billion transistors, 80GB of HBM3 memory (much faster than standard RAM), and hardware designed for mixed-precision arithmetic. It includes Transformer Engine cores that accelerate FP8 tensor operations and NVLink 4.0 interconnect allowing 900 GB/s communication between chips. During training, gradient computation and parameter updates happen on these cores; the fat memory bus keeps data flowing fast enough to saturate them. Multiple H100s link via NVLink to act as a single logical accelerator for distributed training.
why_it_matters: H100 cost and availability directly determine training budget and timeline for any frontier model. If you're buying compute in 2024–2026, H100 rental costs set your per-token training expense. Understanding H100's specs (memory bandwidth, TFLOPs, interconnect) tells you why certain optimization strategies (like flash-attention or tensor parallelism) became essential.
example: A team training a 70B-parameter model on H100 clusters uses tensor parallelism across 8 chips and gradient accumulation to fit batches in 80GB memory. At 3 TB/s bandwidth, they can sustain ~300 TFLOP/s of effective compute per chip. Training for 3 trillion tokens takes ~90 days on 256 H100s; the same job on 8 A100s would take 18 months.
pitfalls: Assuming 80GB is enough for large model training—you almost always need gradient checkpointing and quantization too.
Comparing raw TFLOP ratings across architectures without accounting for memory bandwidth; H100 excels only if data flows fast enough to keep cores busy.
Not accounting for NVLink vs. Ethernet interconnect costs when budgeting multi-node clusters; bandwidth collapse kills scaling efficiency.
Treating H100 as a permanent solution—B200 (Blackwell) offers 5–10× throughput and is entering production; pricing and availability will shift mid-2025.
when_use: Use H100 when training frontier models (70B+ parameters), when you need 80GB GPU memory per card, or when rental cost per token matters more than upfront infrastructure investment.
when_avoid: Avoid H100 for inference at scale (use L40S or specialized inference hardware); for small models under 7B (A100 or even consumer GPUs suffice); or if B200/newer hardware is available in your region at competitive pricing.

faq.q: What's the difference between H100 and A100?
faq.a: H100 has 2× the memory (80GB vs. 40GB), 3× the memory bandwidth (3 TB/s vs. 1.9 TB/s), and Transformer Engine hardware for FP8 ops. A100 is cheaper on the spot market but slower for modern large models.

faq.q: Can I train a 70B model on a single H100?
faq.a: No—a single H100's 80GB cannot fit model weights, activations, and gradients simultaneously. You need gradient checkpointing, quantization, or distributed training across multiple cards.

faq.q: How much does it cost to rent an H100 per hour?
faq.a: On-demand pricing ranges $2–3.50/hour on Lambda Labs, Runpod, or Paperspace; spot pricing is $0.50–1.20/hour. Exact costs vary by region and provider; always check current rates.

faq.q: Is H100 still worth buying in 2025?
faq.a: Probably not for new deployments—B200 enters production mid-2025 with better specs and newer software support. H100 remains viable for existing clusters or cost-constrained inference.

faq.q: Do I need NVLink to connect H100s together?
faq.a: For training, yes—NVLink 4.0 (900 GB/s) is essential for reasonable scaling. Without it, PCIe or Ethernet bottlenecks make multi-GPU training painfully slow on models over 20B parameters.

title: HBM (High Bandwidth Memory)
tldr: Stacked DRAM on GPU delivering 300–1200 GB/s per stack; limits LLM inference throughput.
plain_english: HBM is memory stacked vertically on top of a GPU chip, connected by thousands of tiny wires. Think of it as a building with many floors instead of a single wide floor—you get more bandwidth from the same footprint. It's the reason data-center GPUs cost what they do and why they're hard to buy.
how_it_works: HBM uses DRAM dies stacked 12 layers high, connected via a silicon interposer with microbump pins. Each generation delivers more bandwidth: HBM2 ≈307 GB/s per stack, HBM3 ≈819 GB/s, HBM3e ≈1.2 TB/s. An H100 has 5 stacks (≈3 TB/s total); a B200 has 8 stacks (≈8 TB/s). The interposer is the physical bottleneck—yields and manufacturing complexity drive GPU costs and limit production.
why_it_matters: LLM decode performance is almost entirely bandwidth-bound. GPU memory bandwidth directly predicts tokens-per-second on inference workloads. Without HBM, GPUs would need prohibitively wide memory buses or much slower GDDR, making LLM inference impractical at scale.
example: An H100 can sustain ~2000 tokens/sec on dense matrix operations, but its 3 TB/s memory bandwidth caps batch-1 LLM decode at ~150 tokens/sec. A B200 with 8 TB/s HBM3e can push this to ~400 tokens/sec on the same model. Teams evaluating inference hardware multiply expected batch size by model size, then check HBM bandwidth specs to predict real throughput.
pitfalls: Confusing peak HBM bandwidth with usable bandwidth—DRAM access patterns and prefetch efficiency reduce real throughput by 30–50%.
Ignoring HBM-to-L2 cache bottlenecks; bandwidth doesn't help if compute stalls waiting for data placement.
Assuming HBM supply will improve quickly; yield rates on stacked DRAM are stuck at 40–60%, making HBM the constraint on GPU production, not chip logic.
Treating all HBM stacks equally; HBM3e uses different voltage and thermal profiles, affecting power budgets and sustained throughput.
when_use: Use HBM specs to predict inference throughput when comparing GPUs, or when planning batching strategy for LLM serving.
when_avoid: Avoid relying on peak HBM bandwidth alone—always measure with real workloads, as memory layout and prefetch efficiency vary 2–3x across models.

faq.q: Is HBM the same as VRAM?
faq.a: HBM is a type of VRAM. VRAM (video RAM) is any GPU-attached memory; older GPUs used GDDR5/6/6X instead of HBM. HBM is faster but more expensive and harder to manufacture.

faq.q: Why does HBM affect LLM speed?
faq.a: During decode (single-token generation), the GPU must load the entire model from memory into cache. Higher bandwidth means faster loading, directly increasing tokens/second. Prefill (processing prompt) benefits less because it's more compute-bound.

faq.q: Can you upgrade or add HBM to a GPU after purchase?
faq.a: No. HBM is soldered to the GPU die before packaging. You cannot upgrade, expand, or replace it. GPU choice is final.

faq.q: What's the difference between HBM2, HBM3, and HBM3e?
faq.a: HBM3 doubles HBM2 bandwidth (~819 vs 307 GB/s). HBM3e adds higher density and voltage efficiency on HBM3 (≈1.2 TB/s). Later versions also support faster clocks and lower power.

faq.q: Why is HBM so expensive?
faq.a: HBM requires precision stacking of 12 DRAM dies, advanced packaging, and custom silicon interposers. Yield rates are 40–60% due to defects in any layer, so scrap rates are high and material costs are absorbed into each working unit.
